tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: Extrapolated from cervical cancer trials; GOG 205 (Moore et al. 2012) for locally advanced vulvar cancer
trial_data.endpoints: Pathologic complete response (pCR) 64% clinical, 78% pathologic; 5-year OS 52% (van Triest et al. 2021)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin is the preferred radiosensitizing agent for concurrent chemoradiation in vulvar cancer, extrapolated from efficacy in cervical and anal cancers. The GOG 205 phase II trial demonstrated a pathologic complete response (pCR) rate of 78% in patients with T3/T4 tumors initially unresectable by radical vulvectomy, with 64% clinical complete response. The regimen is also used in the adjuvant setting for node-positive disease (category 1 for ≥2 positive LNs or extranodal extension). Concurrent chemotherapy with EBRT is recommended for locally advanced unresectable disease and for selected recurrences.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin is an alternative radiosensitizing agent for patients who are intolerant to cisplatin. This recommendation is extrapolated from experience in cervical cancer, where carboplatin has shown noninferiority to cisplatin in combination regimens for metastatic disease (JCOG0505). No specific trial data for carboplatin chemoradiation in vulvar cancer are available. Carboplatin is typically used with AUC-based dosing.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: NRG Oncology/GOG Study 279 (Horowitz et al. 2024)
trial_data.endpoints: Complete pathologic response (CPR) 73% (90% CI 61-83%); 12-month PFS 74% (90% CI 62.2-82.7%); 24-month OS 70% (90% CI 57-79%)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Weekly cisplatin and gemcitabine concurrent with intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) was evaluated in a single-arm phase II study (GOG 279) for patients with locally advanced vulvar cancer not amenable to surgical resection. The regimen demonstrated a 73% complete pathologic response rate with manageable toxicity. This regimen is listed as 'Other Recommended' for chemoradiation in the NCCN guidelines.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: GOG 101 (Montana et al. 2000)
trial_data.endpoints: Local control of nodal disease 97%; primary tumor local control 76%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin/fluorouracil is an established chemoradiation regimen for locally advanced vulvar cancer, derived from cervical and anal cancer trials. The GOG 101 study evaluated preoperative chemoradiation with cisplatin/fluorouracil in patients with N2/N3 nodal disease, showing high nodal control rates and enabling subsequent surgery. DPYD testing is recommended before fluorouracil use.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: Extrapolated from cervical and anal cancer trials (Lorvidhaya et al. 2003)

tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen is included as an alternative when cisplatin and carboplatin are unavailable. It is based on extrapolation from trials in cervical cancer (Lorvidhaya et al.) and anal cancer, where capecitabine and mitomycin have been used concurrently with radiation. DPYD testing is recommended before capecitabine use. The guideline recommends this only if single-agent cisplatin or carboplatin are unavailable.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Gemcitabine (Chemoradiation, Single-Agent Alternative if Platinum Unavailable)
tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II study in cervical cancer (Pattaranutaporn et al. 2001)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent gemcitabine as a radiosensitizer is included as an option when cisplatin and carboplatin are unavailable. This recommendation is extrapolated from phase II data in locally advanced cervical cancer. No specific vulvar cancer data are available for this regimen.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Paclitaxel (Chemoradiation, Single-Agent Alternative if Platinum Unavailable)
tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: Pilot study in cervical cancer (Cerrotta et al. 2002)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent paclitaxel is included as an alternative radiosensitizer when cisplatin and carboplatin are unavailable. This is based on early-phase trials in cervical cancer showing efficacy and tolerability with concurrent radiation. No vulvar-specific data exist.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-826 (Colombo et al. 2021) - extrapolated from cervical cancer
trial_data.endpoints: Improved overall survival (OS) and progression-free survival (PFS) in cervical cancer

tier_1_regimens.notes: This regimen is a preferred first-line option for advanced or recurrent/metastatic vulvar cancer, extrapolated from the KEYNOTE-826 trial in cervical cancer. The trial demonstrated a significant OS and PFS benefit with the addition of pembrolizumab to cisplatin/paclitaxel with or without bevacizumab. Pembrolizumab may be continued as maintenance therapy. The guideline notes that checkpoint inhibitors and bevacizumab may be continued as maintenance per original study protocols. Pembrolizumab subcutaneous injection (with berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph) may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-826 and JCOG0505 - extrapolated from cervical cancer

tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin-based alternative to cisplatin for first-line therapy, extrapolated from the KEYNOTE-826 trial (cervical cancer) and JCOG0505 showing noninferiority of carboplatin/paclitaxel. This regimen is preferred when cisplatin is not suitable. Pembrolizumab and bevacizumab may be continued as maintenance therapy. Carboplatin dosing is typically AUC-based.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: GOG 240 (Tewari et al. 2017) - extrapolated from cervical cancer
trial_data.endpoints: Improved OS (HR 0.71, 95% CI 0.55-0.91) in cervical cancer

tier_1_regimens.notes: Bevacizumab in combination with cisplatin/paclitaxel is a preferred first-line option for advanced/recurrent/metastatic vulvar cancer, extrapolated from the GOG 240 phase III trial in cervical cancer which demonstrated an OS benefit. Bevacizumab may be continued as maintenance therapy. This regimen is recommended for patients who are candidates for anti-angiogenic therapy.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)
tier_1_regimens.notes: A carboplatin-based alternative to cisplatin/paclitaxel/bevacizumab, extrapolated from cervical cancer data. This regimen is preferred for patients who cannot tolerate cisplatin. Bevacizumab may be continued as maintenance therapy. Carboplatin dosing is typically AUC-based.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: Extrapolated from cervical cancer phase III trials (GOG 240, etc.)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin/paclitaxel doublet is a preferred first-line regimen for advanced/recurrent/metastatic vulvar cancer without addition of biologic agents. This combination has demonstrated efficacy in cervical cancer and is commonly used in vulvar cancer based on extrapolation. A small series (Han et al. 2012) noted limited clinical benefit of carboplatin/paclitaxel in vulvar cancer, but the combination is still recommended.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Carboplatin/paclitaxel is a preferred first-line alternative for patients who cannot tolerate cisplatin, extrapolated from cervical cancer data (JCOG0505). This regimen is commonly used due to its favorable toxicity profile. A small series in vulvar cancer (Han et al. 2012) included only 6 patients, with limited clinical benefit reported, but the regimen remains recommended based on cervical cancer data.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent cisplatin is listed as an 'Other Recommended' first-line option for advanced/recurrent/metastatic vulvar cancer. This may be used in patients who are not candidates for combination chemotherapy. The recommendation is based on extrapolation from cervical cancer and small vulvar cancer series.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent carboplatin is an 'Other Recommended' first-line option for advanced/recurrent/metastatic disease, typically used when cisplatin is contraindicated. The recommendation is extrapolated from cervical cancer where carboplatin has shown activity as a single agent.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: EORTC-GCG phase II trial (Witteveen et al. 2009)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Single-agent paclitaxel was evaluated in a phase II trial of 31 patients with advanced, recurrent, or metastatic vulvar cancer not amenable to surgery or radiotherapy. The response rate was 14% with a median PFS of 2.6 months, demonstrating modest activity. This regimen is listed as an 'Other Recommended' second-line or subsequent therapy option.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: Empower-Cervical-1 (Tewari et al. 2022); also phase II in cutaneous SCC (Migden et al. 2018)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Cemiplimab is a PD-1 inhibitor recommended as a second-line or subsequent therapy option for advanced/recurrent/metastatic vulvar cancer. The recommendation is extrapolated from the phase III Empower-Cervical-1 trial in recurrent cervical cancer, which showed significant OS and PFS improvement versus chemotherapy. Cemiplimab also showed efficacy in advanced cutaneous SCC. The guideline includes this as an 'Other Recommended' regimen.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: Phase II trial (Horowitz et al. 2012)
trial_data.endpoints: Partial responses 27.5%; stable disease 40%; short-duration responses

tier_1_regimens.notes: Erlotinib, an EGFR tyrosine kinase inhibitor, was studied in a phase II trial in patients with metastatic vulvar cancer. The trial reported partial responses in 27.5% and stable disease in 40% of patients, but responses were short-lived. This is a category 2B recommendation, indicating NCCN consensus with lower-level evidence. Erlotinib is listed as an 'Other Recommended' second-line or subsequent therapy.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)
tier_1_regimens.notes: Cisplatin/gemcitabine combination is listed as a 'Other Recommended' second-line or subsequent therapy option with a category 2B designation. This is extrapolated from cervical cancer data (GOG 204), but case reports in vulvar cancer (Santeufemia et al. 2012) have shown mixed results. The lower evidence level reflects the lack of robust vulvar-specific data.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: CheckMate 358 (Oaknin et al. 2024) - cervical cancer cohort; CheckMate 848 (Schenker et al. 2024) - TMB-H solid tumors

tier_1_regimens.notes: The combination of ipilimumab (CTLA-4 inhibitor) and nivolumab (PD-1 inhibitor) was added in version 1.2026 as a second-line or subsequent therapy option. This is based on the CheckMate 358 trial in recurrent or metastatic cervical cancer and the CheckMate 848 trial in TMB-H solid tumors. The guideline lists this as an 'Other Recommended' regimen. Nivolumab subcutaneous injection (with hyaluronidase-nvhy) may be substituted for IV nivolumab.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

biomarkers.requirement: Must be ≥10 mutations/megabase as determined by an FDA-approved assay or validated test

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-158 (Marabelle et al. 2020)
trial_data.endpoints: ORR 10.9% overall; median PFS 2.1 months; median OS 6.2 months; in TMB-H subgroup ORR ~17%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is recommended for treatment of unresectable or metastatic TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb) solid tumors that have progressed following prior treatment with no satisfactory alternative options. The KEYNOTE-158 study demonstrated an ORR of 10.9% in the vulvar SCC cohort, with higher response in TMB-H tumors (~17%). The FDA has granted tissue-agnostic approval for this indication. Pembrolizumab subcutaneous injection (with berahyaluronidase alfa-pmph) may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-158 (Shapira-Frommer et al. 2022)
trial_data.endpoints: ORR 9.5% in PD-L1-positive population; median PFS 2.1 months; median OS 6.2 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is recommended for disease progression on or after chemotherapy in patients whose tumors express PD-L1 with CPS ≥1. The KEYNOTE-158 vulvar cancer cohort showed an ORR of 9.5% in PD-L1-positive patients. This is a tissue-agnostic FDA indication for PD-L1-positive tumors. Pembrolizumab subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

biomarkers.marker: MSI-H (Microsatellite Instability-High) or dMMR (Mismatch Repair Deficient)
biomarkers.requirement: Positive for MSI-H or dMMR by validated test

trial_data.trial_name: KEYNOTE-158 (Marabelle et al. 2020)
trial_data.endpoints: ORR 34.3% across all tumor types; median PFS 4.1 months; median OS 23.5 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Pembrolizumab is FDA-approved for MSI-H/dMMR solid tumors that have progressed after prior therapy, regardless of tumor type. The KEYNOTE-158 study included 233 patients with non-colorectal MSI-H/dMMR tumors (one vulvar cancer) and showed an ORR of 34.3%. This is a tissue-agnostic indication. Pembrolizumab subcutaneous injection may be substituted for IV pembrolizumab.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

biomarkers.requirement: IHC 3+ or 2+ (with reflex FISH if equivocal)

trial_data.trial_name: DESTINY-PanTumor02 (Meric-Bernstam et al. 2024)
trial_data.endpoints: ORR 37.1% overall; in cervical cancer ORR 50% (HER2 3+ ORR 75%); median PFS 6.9 months; median OS 13.4 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (T-DXd) is an antibody-drug conjugate targeting HER2. The DESTINY-PanTumor02 trial showed clinically meaningful activity in HER2-expressing solid tumors, including cervical cancer (ORR 50%, with 75% in HER2 IHC3+). The NCCN guidelines recommend HER2 IHC testing (with reflex FISH) for advanced or recurrent/metastatic vulvar cancer. T-DXd is listed as a 'Useful in Certain Circumstances' second-line or subsequent therapy.

tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

biomarkers.requirement: HPV-positive tumor or unknown HPV status

trial_data.trial_name: CheckMate 358 (Naumann et al. 2019)
trial_data.n_patients: 5 (vaginal or vulvar cancer, HPV-positive or unknown)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Nivolumab monotherapy was evaluated in the CheckMate 358 trial which included a small cohort of 5 patients with recurrent or metastatic vaginal or vulvar cancer who were HPV-positive or had unknown HPV status. The 12-month OS rate was 40%. Based on these limited data, nivolumab is included as a 'Useful in Certain Circumstances' option for HPV-related advanced vulvar cancer. Nivolumab subcutaneous injection (with hyaluronidase-nvhy) may be substituted for IV nivolumab.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Larotrectinib (NTRK Gene Fusion-Positive, Second-Line or Subsequent)
tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

biomarkers.requirement: Positive for NTRK1/2/3 fusion by validated test

trial_data.trial_name: Pooled analysis of three phase 1/2 trials (Drilon et al. 2018; Hong et al. 2020)
trial_data.endpoints: ORR 79% (95% CI 72-85); complete response 16%; median duration of response 35.2 months; median PFS 28.3 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Larotrectinib is a TRK inhibitor approved for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors regardless of histology. The pooled analysis of three clinical trials showed a high response rate (79%) with durable responses in patients with NTRK gene fusions. The NCCN guidelines recommend this for NTRK-positive tumors in the second-line or subsequent setting, and it may also be used for treatment-naïve patients. Comprehensive molecular profiling should include NTRK testing.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Entrectinib (NTRK Gene Fusion-Positive, Second-Line or Subsequent)
tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

biomarkers.requirement: Positive for NTRK1/2/3 fusion by validated test

trial_data.trial_name: Integrated analysis of three phase 1/2 trials (Doebele et al. 2020; long-term follow-up 2022)
trial_data.endpoints: ORR 61%; median duration of response 20 months (95% CI 10.1-19.9); median PFS 11.2 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: Entrectinib is a TRK inhibitor approved for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors. The integrated analysis of three phase 1/2 trials showed an ORR of 61% with a median PFS of 11.2 months in NTRK fusion-positive tumors. Entrectinib also has CNS activity. The NCCN recommends it as a 'Useful in Certain Circumstances' option for NTRK-positive advanced vulvar cancer in the second-line or subsequent setting.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Repotrectinib (NTRK Gene Fusion-Positive, Second-Line or Subsequent)
tier_1_regimens.site: Vulvar Cancer (Squamous Cell Carcinoma and Adenocarcinoma)

biomarkers.requirement: Positive for NTRK1/2/3 fusion; may be used in TRK TKI-naïve patients or those who progressed on prior NTRK therapy

trial_data.trial_name: TRIDENT-1 trial (Solomon et al. 2023)
trial_data.endpoints: ORR 58% (95% CI 41-73); median duration of response not estimable; 12-month duration of response rate 86%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Repotrectinib is a next-generation TRK inhibitor granted accelerated approval for NTRK fusion-positive solid tumors. The TRIDENT-1 trial showed an ORR of 58% in TRK TKI-naïve patients with a high 12-month duration of response rate (86%). This is included as a 'Useful in Certain Circumstances' option for NTRK-positive tumors that are naïve to prior NTRK-targeted therapy or have progressed on prior NTRK therapy.

definition.squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino: Vulvar cancer is a malignant neoplasm arising from the vulvar epithelium, most commonly squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), which accounts for 90% of cases [7]. Adenocarcinoma of the vulva is rarer and typically arises from Bartholin glands or other adnexal structures [9]. The vulva develops from the urogenital sinus and ectoderm, and its anatomical boundaries include the labia majora, labia minora, clitoris, mons pubis, and perineum. The majority of vulvar cancers are located in the labia majora, though other sites include the labia minora, clitoris, mons pubis, or perineum [19,27]. In HPV-associated tumors, multifocal lesions and concurrent cervical neoplasia are common, while HPV-independent tumors often present as a single mass or ulcer [19,20]. Vulvar cancer represents 5%–8% of all gynecologic malignancies [1]. The malignancy is staged using the 2021 FIGO staging system, which incorporates findings from cross-sectional imaging and revised definitions for depth of invasion and lymph node metastases [22]. Depth of invasion is now measured from the basement membrane of the deepest adjacent dysplastic rete ridge to the deepest point of invasion [22]. Stage IA disease is defined as tumor ≤2 cm with stromal invasion ≤1 mm; stage IB includes tumor >2 cm or invasion >1 mm. Higher stages involve extension to adjacent structures, nodal metastases, or distant spread [22, ST-1]. Histologic high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (HSIL, formerly VIN 2/3) can be treated with wide local excision [VULVA-1].
definition.vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma: Vulvar and vulvovaginal melanomas are rare melanomas arising from melanocytes in the vulvar skin or mucosa. Cutaneous vulvar melanoma is defined as lesions occurring on the vulvar vestibule outside Hart's line, while mucosal vulvovaginal melanoma occurs inside Hart's line [VM-1A]. Vulvar melanoma (VuM) is the second most common malignant disease of the vulva, after SCC [186]. Primary vaginal malignant melanoma is even rarer, with fewer than 300 reported cases [188]. These melanomas are staged using the AJCC TNM staging system for cutaneous melanoma, and clinical staging includes microstaging of the primary tumor and assessment of nodal involvement [194, VM-1A]. The median age at diagnosis is 68 years, and most occur in postmenopausal individuals [189]. Approximately 38% of patients with vulvar melanoma have advanced disease (regional or distant) at diagnosis [186]. The 5-year overall survival for vulvar melanoma is approximately 47%, significantly lower than the 92% for cutaneous melanoma [190]. Anatomically, the vulva includes the labia, clitoris, and vestibule, and melanomas can arise from any of these structures. Mucosal vulvovaginal melanoma involves the vaginal mucosa and may present with bleeding, discharge, or a mass.

squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.name: HPV-associated squamous cell carcinoma
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.description: HPV-associated SCC is linked to persistent infection with high-risk HPV types, most commonly HPV-16 (78.1% of HPV-positive cases) and HPV-33 (7.5%) [18]. It frequently occurs in younger patients, is often multifocal, and is associated with classic (usual-type) vulvar intraepithelial neoplasia (VIN). It can be seen in conjunction with additional sites of lower genital tract squamous neoplasia [VULVA-A 1 of 4]. Immunohistochemistry (IHC) shows strong, diffuse, block-like positive nuclear and cytoplasmic staining with p16 and wild-type p53 (heterogeneous staining pattern and basal sparing pattern) [VULVA-A 1 of 4,5].
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.frequency: Approximately 39–40% of vulvar SCCs are HPV-associated, with a meta-analysis reporting HPV prevalence of 39.1% in invasive cancer and 76.1% in VIN [18]. p16 positivity is seen in 34.1% of vulvar cancers [18].
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.key_associations: HPV infection (especially HPV-16 and HPV-18), younger age at diagnosis, multifocal disease, classic VIN, concurrent cervical neoplasia, better prognosis compared to HPV-independent SCC [VULVA-A 1 of 4,19].

squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.name: HPV-independent squamous cell carcinoma
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.description: HPV-independent SCC arises in the setting of chronic vulvar inflammatory disorders, most commonly lichen sclerosus [VULVA-A 1 of 4]. It is split into two main groups: those associated with TP53 mutations (p53 abnormal) and those with wild-type TP53 status (p53 wild-type) [13]. The p53 abnormal subtype usually occurs in older patients, is unifocal, and is associated with differentiated VIN (dVIN). IHC usually shows aberrant p53 staining (widespread, strong nuclear expression or complete absence/null expression) and patchy (negative) p16 staining [VULVA-A 1 of 4,5]. The p53 wild-type group has intermediate clinical outcomes [13].
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.frequency: HPV-independent SCC accounts for approximately 60% of vulvar SCCs [18]. Of these, the majority have aberrant p53 expression (TP53 mutant), though precise frequency varies by study [13].
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.key_associations: Lichen sclerosus, older age, unifocal disease, differentiated VIN (dVIN), TP53 mutations (aberrant p53), worst clinical outcomes among the three molecular categories (HPV-positive, HPV-negative/p53 mutant, HPV-negative/p53 wild-type) [13, VULVA-A 1 of 4].

vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.name: Cutaneous vulvar melanoma
vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.description: Cutaneous vulvar melanoma arises from melanocytes in the vulvar skin outside Hart's line (the vestibule border) [VM-1A]. It shares similar immunohistochemical markers with cutaneous melanoma (S100B, HMB45, Melan-A) but has distinct molecular features, including rare BRAF V600 mutations in some studies [191,192]. It is staged according to the AJCC melanoma staging system [194].
vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.frequency: Annual incidence <0.2 per 100,000 individuals assigned female at birth [187]. Vulvar melanoma accounts for 7% of all melanomas in women and 5% of vulvar malignancies [185,186].
vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.key_associations: BRAF mutations (variable frequency, up to 50% in some series), KIT mutations, NRAS mutations, PD-L1 expression. Worse prognosis than cutaneous melanoma with 5-year OS ~47% [190].

vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.name: Mucosal vulvovaginal melanoma
vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.description: Mucosal vulvovaginal melanoma occurs on the vulvar vestibule inside Hart's line, involving the vaginal mucosa [VM-1A]. It is a rare entity with distinct molecular features compared to cutaneous melanoma, including frequent KIT amplifications and NRAS mutations, but rare BRAF mutations [192,193]. Management often involves wide local excision with clear margins, and consideration of sentinel lymph node biopsy and adjuvant radiation [VM-2].
vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.frequency: Incidence approximately 0.46 cases per million individuals per year [188]. Fewer than 300 cases reported in the literature [188].
vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.key_associations: KIT mutations/amplifications (22% in one series), NRAS mutations, rare BRAF mutations. Higher rates of PD-L1 expression. Often presents at advanced stage due to delay in diagnosis [190,192].

molecular_pathogenesis.squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino: Vulvar squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) develops through two distinct molecular pathways: HPV-associated and HPV-independent [VULVA-A 1 of 4]. The HPV-associated pathway is driven by persistent infection with high-risk HPV types (most commonly HPV-16 and HPV-33) [18,19]. Viral oncoproteins E6 and E7 inactivate p53 and Rb, leading to genomic instability and overexpression of p16INK4a, detected by diffuse block-like p16 immunohistochemistry. These tumors typically retain wild-type p53 [VULVA-A 1 of 4]. The HPV-independent pathway is associated with chronic inflammatory conditions such as lichen sclerosus and is characterized by TP53 mutations in a majority of cases [13, VULVA-A 1 of 4]. Aberrant p53 staining (either null or overexpression) is seen in IHC, while p16 staining is patchy or negative [5,52]. TP53 mutations are found in approximately 60–80% of HPV-independent vulvar SCCs, leading to the worst clinical outcomes among the three molecular categories: HPV-positive, HPV-negative/p53 mutant, and HPV-negative/p53 wild-type [13]. Additional molecular alterations in vulvar SCC include occasional mutations in CDKN2A, PIK3CA, and NOTCH1, though comprehensive genomic profiling is not routinely performed unless for biomarker-directed therapy [VULVA-E]. For advanced or recurrent disease, testing for PD-L1, HER2, mismatch repair (MMR)/microsatellite instability (MSI), tumor mutational burden (TMB), and NTRK gene fusions is recommended to identify potential targeted therapy opportunities [VULVA-A 2 of 4]. Approximately 10–50% of vulvar cancers express PD-L1 [168,169]. HER2 overexpression (IHC 3+ or 2+) can be found in a subset and may be targeted with trastuzumab deruxtecan [178]. TMB-high (≥10 mut/Mb) and MSI-H/dMMR tumors are rare but respond to pembrolizumab [174,175].
molecular_pathogenesis.vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma: Vulvar and vulvovaginal melanomas have distinct molecular profiles compared to cutaneous melanomas. BRAF V600 mutations are less common; one study reported no BRAF mutations in vulvar melanomas, while another found BRAF mutations in 50% of vulvovaginal melanoma tumors (compared to 82.1% in non-gynecologic melanomas) [192,193]. KIT mutations and amplifications are more frequent, occurring in 22% of vulvovaginal melanomas [192]. NRAS mutations are also seen in both vulvar and vaginal melanomas [193]. PD-L1 expression is significantly more common in vulvovaginal melanoma than in cutaneous melanoma [192]. Other frequent alterations include mutations in cell regulatory proteins such as c-KIT and amplifications in KIT [191,192]. The mutational landscape suggests that vulvovaginal melanoma is a unique subtype of mucosal melanoma. Somatic mutation testing for BRAF and KIT is clinically indicated to guide targeted therapy options [VM-1A]. Additionally, comprehensive molecular profiling for TMB, MSI, and NTRK fusions may identify rare pan-tumor therapy opportunities [VM-1A, VULVA-A 2 of 4].

squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.incidence: In 2025, an estimated 7,480 new cases of vulvar cancer are expected in the United States [1]. The disease accounts for 5%–8% of all gynecologic malignancies [1]. The overall age-adjusted incidence is approximately 2.5 per 100,000 women per year [4].
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.mortality: Approximately 1,770 deaths from vulvar cancer are expected in 2025 [1]. Five-year relative survival rates from SEER data (2015–2021) are 85.7% for localized disease (stages I/II), 49.71% for regional/locally advanced disease (stages III/IVA), and 21.9% for stage IVB (distant metastases) [4].
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.trends: From 2001 to 2017, the incidence of HPV-associated vulvar cancer increased by 1.2% per year, most notably among individuals aged 50–59 (2.6% per year), 60–69 (2.4%), and ≥70 (0.9%). The overall incidence of non-HPV vulvar cancer remained stable [21]. As HPV vaccination coverage increases, continued surveillance is needed to monitor trends [21].
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.demographics: Median age at diagnosis is 68 years [1]. Treatment approaches vary considerably by sociodemographic factors: race/ethnicity, age, and non-private insurance are associated with disparities in treatment and survival, particularly for advanced disease [5,6]. Most vulvar cancers are diagnosed at early stages [8].

vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.incidence: Vulvar melanoma has an annual incidence of less than 0.2 per 100,000 individuals assigned female at birth (AFAB) [187]. Primary vaginal malignant melanoma is even rarer, with an incidence of 0.46 cases per million per year [188]. Vulvar melanoma accounts for 7% of all melanomas in women and 5% of all vulvar malignancies [185,186].
vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.mortality: Five-year overall survival for vulvar melanoma is approximately 47%, compared to 92% for cutaneous melanoma [190]. This poor survival is partly due to delayed diagnosis. Survival has not improved significantly over time [190].
vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.trends: No specific temporal trends were identified in the source, but the incidence of vulvar melanoma appears stable. Advances in systemic therapy (immunotherapy and targeted therapy) may improve outcomes in advanced stages [VM-3].
vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.demographics: Median age at diagnosis is 68 years, and it most commonly occurs in postmenopausal individuals [186,189]. Approximately 38% of patients with staged vulvar melanoma have advanced disease (regional or distant) at diagnosis; mean tumor size at diagnosis is 31 mm and >46% have Breslow thickness >2 mm [186].

squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.detail: The median age at diagnosis is 68 years; risk increases with age [1,19].

Persistent infection with high-risk HPV types, especially HPV-16 (78.1% of HPV-positive cases) and HPV-33 (7.5%), is strongly associated with HPV-associated vulvar SCC [18]. HPV prevalence in vulvar cancer is approximately 39–40% [18].
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.strength: Strong causal association; approximately 40% of vulvar cancers attributable to HPV

squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.detail: Smoking is a recognized risk factor for the development of vulvar neoplasia [19, VULVA-1]. Smoking cessation is recommended [VULVA-1, VULVA-8].
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.strength: Moderate to strong association; risk increases with duration and intensity

squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.detail: HPV-independent VIN and SCC are identified in the setting of chronic vulvar inflammatory disorders such as lichen sclerosus [VULVA-A 1 of 4]. Differentiated VIN (dVIN) is associated with lichen sclerosus and has a faster rate of progression to invasive SCC [MS-2].
Immunosuppression (including HIV) increases risk of vulvar neoplasia. Consider HIV testing, especially in younger patients with SCC vulva [VULVA-1].
squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino.strength: Established risk factor; higher risk in HIV-positive populations

vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.detail: Median age at diagnosis is 68 years; most cases occur in postmenopausal individuals [189].
vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.strength: Established; increasing age is a risk factor

vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.detail: No specific genetic syndromes or family history are mentioned in the source, but somatic mutations (KIT, BRAF, NRAS) are common [192,193].
vulvar_and_vulvovaginal_melanoma.strength: Not quantified; likely smaller role compared to cutaneous melanoma

squamous_cell_carcinoma_and_adenocarcino: HPV vaccination: Vaccination with currently available HPV vaccines (targeting HPV-16 and HPV-18) may reduce the burden of HPV-related vulvar cancers in the future [14,19].
Smoking cessation: Avoidance of smoking reduces risk of vulvar neoplasia [VULVA-1, VULVA-8].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Vulvar cancer typically presents in the labia majora, labia minora, clitoris, mons pubis, or perineum. In HPV-independent (p53 abnormal) tumors, the classic presentation is a single mass or ulcer on the labia majora or minora, often in older patients (median age 68 years) with chronic vulvar inflammatory disorders such as lichen sclerosus. In HPV-associated tumors, multifocal lesions and concurrent cervical neoplasia are more common, and patients are frequently younger. Many patients are asymptomatic at early stages, but when symptoms occur, pruritus and pain/irritation are most common; vulvar bleeding or discharge may also occur. A majority of patients present with early-stage localized disease (stage I/II), with 5-year survival of 85.7% for localized disease based on SEER data (2015–2021).

symptoms.name: Pruritus
symptoms.detail: Itching is a common presenting symptom, often chronic and localized to the vulvar area.

symptoms.name: Pain/irritation
symptoms.detail: Localized pain, burning, or irritation of the vulva, often associated with ulceration or advanced disease.
symptoms.alarm: Progressive pain unresponsive to conservative measures

symptoms.name: Vulvar bleeding or discharge
symptoms.detail: Spontaneous bleeding or serosanguinous discharge from a visible lesion.
symptoms.alarm: New onset bleeding in postmenopausal women

symptoms.name: Palpable mass
symptoms.detail: A single lump or nodule, often on the labia majora, sometimes ulcerated.

signs.name: Visible vulvar lesion
signs.detail: May appear as an exophytic mass, ulcer, or area of leukoplakia/erythroplakia. In HPV-associated cases, multiple lesions often present.

signs.name: Inguinal lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Palpable enlarged groin lymph nodes, may be fixed or ulcerated in advanced disease.
signs.frequency: Up to 30% at presentation, higher in stage III/IV

signs.name: Lichen sclerosus or differentiated VIN
signs.detail: Signs of chronic inflammatory vulvar conditions, especially in HPV-independent disease; may appear as white plaque or atrophic skin.

signs.name: Multifocal lesions
signs.detail: Multiple separate lesions suggest HPV-associated disease; may involve vagina and cervix.

atypical_or_missed: Asymptomatic early-stage disease may be missed; many patients are diagnosed incidentally during routine exam or cervical screening.
Lesions mistaken for benign conditions such as lichen sclerosus, dermatitis, or viral warts, delaying biopsy.
HPV-independent vulvar cancer in older patients often presents as a unifocal ulcer that may be attributed to trauma or infection.
Vulvar melanoma (both cutaneous and mucosal) may present as a pigmented or amelanotic lesion, often misdiagnosed as a benign nevus or vascular lesion.
In young women with HPV-related disease, multifocal high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesions (HSIL) may be overlooked or treated as genital warts.

red_flags: Persistent vulvar pruritus or pain lasting more than 4 weeks despite conservative therapy
Any visible vulvar mass, ulcer, or area of mucosal irregularity that fails to heal within 2–3 weeks
Palpable inguinal or femoral lymphadenopathy, especially if firm, fixed, or >1.5 cm
Unexplained vulvar bleeding or discharge in a postmenopausal patient
Rapid growth of a vulvar lesion
Multiple vulvar lesions, particularly with history of cervical or vaginal HPV-related neoplasia
History of lichen sclerosus with new or changing lesion
Symptoms of urinary obstruction or tenesmus from locally advanced disease

diagnostic.rationale: Comprehensive evaluation of vulvar, vaginal, cervical, and inguinal regions; assess for multifocal disease and nodal involvement.
diagnostic.key_findings: Visible lesion characteristics (size, location, multiplicity), inguinal node palpation, assessment of vaginal/cervical involvement.

diagnostic.rationale: Definitive diagnosis; must include adequate sampling of suspicious areas, preferably with punch or excisional biopsy.
diagnostic.key_findings: Confirms squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) or adenocarcinoma; distinguishes HPV-associated vs. HPV-independent by p16 and p53 IHC; reports histologic type, depth of invasion (mm), LVSI, margin status.

diagnostic.rationale: Baseline assessment for anemia, infection, or thrombocytosis; part of routine workup.
diagnostic.key_findings: May show anemia from chronic disease or bleeding.

diagnostic.rationale: Baseline for chemotherapy planning and assessment of metastatic disease.
diagnostic.key_findings: Abnormal LFTs may suggest hepatic metastases; renal function needed for platinum-based chemotherapy dosing.

diagnostic.rationale: Due to multifocal lower genital tract neoplasia risk; HPV testing helps identify HPV-associated vulvar disease.
diagnostic.key_findings: Positive HPV (especially HPV-16, HPV-33) supports HPV-associated pathway; concurrent cervical intraepithelial neoplasia may be present.

diagnostic.rationale: Recommended especially in younger patients with vulvar SCC or other HPV-related disease; HIV status affects management but cancer treatment should not be modified solely based on HIV.
diagnostic.key_findings: Positive HIV; refer to HIV specialist.

diagnostic.test: Examination under anesthesia (EUA) with cystoscopy or proctoscopy
diagnostic.rationale: Indicated when tumor involves or is near urethra, bladder, or anus; helps delineate extent for surgical planning.
diagnostic.key_findings: Direct visualization of mucosal involvement; may guide need for ureteral stents or colostomy.

diagnostic.rationale: Recommended at all stages to identify psychosocial needs including social determinants of health.
diagnostic.key_findings: Scores identify need for referral to supportive care services.

staging.rationale: Consider to aid in surgical and/or radiation treatment planning; best for assessing local tumor extent, depth of invasion, and involvement of adjacent structures (urethra, vagina, anus).
staging.indication: Bulky vulvar tumor (≥4 cm or close to critical structures); vaginal, urethral, or anal involvement; delay in presentation/treatment; abnormal physical exam.

staging.test: FDG-PET/CT (neck, chest, abdomen, pelvis, groin) or chest/abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast
staging.rationale: Assess for nodal and distant metastases; PET/CT more sensitive for detecting metastatic disease and aids in radiation planning.
staging.indication: Clinical stage II or higher tumors; suspected metastasis; positive sentinel nodes to evaluate for undissected nodal disease.

staging.rationale: Baseline for pulmonary metastases; if abnormality seen, proceed to chest CT without contrast.
staging.indication: Initial workup for all patients; alternative to chest CT.

staging.rationale: Additional imaging as clinically indicated for symptoms suggestive of metastases (e.g., bone scan for pain, brain MRI for neurological symptoms).

staging.test: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) of radiographically suspicious lymph nodes
staging.rationale: Confirm nodal metastasis when inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy not performed or to guide treatment planning.
staging.indication: Enlarged LNs in locally advanced disease.

staging.test: Intraoperative frozen section of suspicious nodes
staging.rationale: During inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy, to tailor extent and bilaterality of lymphadenectomy.
staging.indication: Grossly enlarged/suspicious nodes identified during surgery.

biomarkers.test: HPV status determination (p16 IHC or RNA sequencing or HPV ISH or DNA sequencing)
biomarkers.rationale: Classify tumor as HPV-associated vs. HPV-independent; HPV-associated has better prognosis.

biomarkers.test: p53 IHC (or NGS for TP53 mutations)
biomarkers.rationale: Determine TP53 status in HPV-negative tumors; p53 abnormal SCC has worst clinical outcomes among three molecular categories.
Assess eligibility for pembrolizumab in recurrent, progressive, or metastatic disease (tumors with CPS ≥1).
biomarkers.when: Consider for patients with recurrent, progressive, or metastatic disease.

biomarkers.test: HER2 IHC with reflex to FISH for equivocal (2+)
biomarkers.rationale: Identify HER2-positive tumors (IHC 3+ or 2+ with FISH positive) for potential treatment with fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki.
biomarkers.when: Recommended for advanced or recurrent/metastatic disease.

biomarkers.test: Comprehensive molecular profiling (FDA-approved or CLIA-validated test including MMR/MSI, TMB, NTRK)
biomarkers.rationale: Identify rare pan-tumor targeted therapy opportunities: MSI-H/dMMR (pembrolizumab), TMB-H (pembrolizumab), NTRK fusions (larotrectinib, entrectinib, repotrectinib).
biomarkers.when: Consider for advanced or recurrent/metastatic disease.

biomarkers.test: Somatic mutational testing for cutaneous vulvar and mucosal vulvovaginal melanoma (BRAF, KIT)
biomarkers.rationale: Identify actionable mutations for targeted therapy in melanoma (e.g., BRAF V600 for dabrafenib/trametinib, KIT mutations).
biomarkers.when: Clinically indicated in vulvar and vulvovaginal melanoma.

do_not_miss: Histologic classification: HPV-associated vs. HPV-independent (p16 and p53 IHC).
Accurate measurement of depth of invasion (from basement membrane of deepest adjacent dysplastic rete ridge to deepest point of invasion per FIGO 2021 method).
Sentinel lymph node ultrastaging for low-volume metastases (requires serial sections and cytokeratin IHC).
For advanced/recurrent disease: PD-L1, HER2, MSI/MMR, TMB, NTRK testing to identify potential targeted or immunotherapy options.
Assessment of distress using the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List.
avoid: Chest CT with contrast if screening chest x-ray is normal and no symptoms (use without contrast if needed).
Repeating imaging without clinical indication; surveillance imaging is symptom or examination-driven.
Routine use of cystoscopy/proctoscopy without clinical suspicion of involvement.
Delaying biopsy of suspicious lesions; non-diagnostic observation is inappropriate.
Modifying cancer treatment solely on basis of HIV status (refer to specialist but treat per guidelines).

staging.system: FIGO 2021 (International Federation of Gynecology and Obstetrics) staging for carcinoma of the vulva; also AJCC 8th edition TNM equivalent.

t_stages.description: Tumor confined to the vulva or perineum. T1a: tumor size ≤2 cm and stromal invasion ≤1 mm. T1b: tumor size >2 cm or stromal invasion >1 mm.
Tumor of any size with extension to lower one-third of the urethra, lower one-third of the vagina, lower one-third of the anus, with negative nodes.

Tumor of any size with extension to upper part of adjacent perineal structures (upper two-thirds of urethra, upper two-thirds of vagina, bladder mucosa, rectal mucosa) or with any number of nonfixed, nonulcerated lymph node metastases.
Tumor of any size fixed to bone (pelvic bone) or with fixed or ulcerated regional lymph node metastases, or distant metastases.

n_stages.description: Regional lymph node metastases ≤5 mm (includes micrometastases and isolated tumor cells).
Regional lymph node metastases with extracapsular spread.
Fixed or ulcerated regional lymph node metastases (IVA) or distant metastases (IVB); note: N4 is not explicitly used; staging includes N0-3 plus M.

m_stages.description: Distant metastases (including pelvic nodal disease beyond inguinofemoral region).

stage_groups.criteria: T1 N0 M0. IA: tumor ≤2 cm and stromal invasion ≤1 mm. IB: tumor >2 cm or stromal invasion >1 mm.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Disease confined to vulva/perineum, node negative. Most common presentation (early stage).
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 85.7% for localized disease (SEER 2015–2021, includes stages I and II together).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; primary surgical resection with or without sentinel node biopsy.

stage_groups.criteria: T2 N0 M0. Tumor of any size with extension to lower one-third of urethra, vagina, or anus, node negative.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locally advanced but still node negative; may require more extensive resection or chemoradiation.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 85.7% for localized disease combined with stage I.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; primary surgery (if resectable) or chemoradiation.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T with N1-N3 M0. IIIA: tumor with extension to upper two-thirds of urethra/vagina/bladder/rectal mucosa OR regional LN metastases ≤5 mm. IIIB: regional LN metastases >5 mm. IIIC: regional LN metastases with extracapsular spread.
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Node-positive disease or significant local extension; high risk of recurrence.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 49.71% for regional/locally advanced (SEER stages III/IVA combined).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative intent with multimodality therapy (surgery, chemoradiation, adjuvant therapy).

stage_groups.criteria: IVA: Disease fixed to pelvic bone OR fixed or ulcerated regional LN metastases. IVB: Distant metastases (M1).
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Advanced disease with bone fixation or non-resectable nodal disease (IVA) or distant spread (IVB).
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 49.71% for stage IVA (combined with stage III in SEER estimate) and 21.9% for stage IVB.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: IVA: curative intent with chemoradiation ± resection. IVB: palliative; systemic therapy ± RT.

staging_pearls: Depth of invasion is measured from the basement membrane of the deepest adjacent dysplastic tumor-free rete ridge to the deepest point of invasion (FIGO 2021 method A), not from the most superficial dermal papilla (previous method).
Stage IIIA includes any number of LNs ≤5 mm; stage IIIB includes any LNs >5 mm. The 5-mm threshold is used for staging, while 2 mm is used for treatment selection.
Regional nodes are inguinal and femoral (not pelvic); pelvic nodal disease is considered distant metastasis (M1) per FIGO 2021.
Imaging (MRI, PET/CT) can be used to assign stage when surgical staging is not performed, especially in unresectable disease.
Stage IIIC (extracapsular spread) is a distinct category with worse prognosis, warranting aggressive adjuvant therapy.
Stage IVA includes disease fixed to pelvic bone OR fixed/ulcerated regional LNs; note that tumor extension to pelvic bone is T4.
The 2021 FIGO revision removed stage IVA for upper urethral/vaginal/bladder/rectal involvement (now IIIA) and reclassified single >5 mm node as IIIB.

management_principles.overview: Vulvar cancer treatment requires a multidisciplinary, individualized approach that integrates surgery, radiation therapy (RT), and systemic therapy, with decisions guided by histology (HPV-associated vs. HPV-independent), stage, tumor location, and nodal status. For squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) and adenocarcinoma, management pathways are stratified into early-stage (Stage I and select Stage II), locally advanced (unresectable without removing proximal urethra/bladder/anus), and metastatic disease beyond the pelvis (Stage IVB). Vulvar and vulvovaginal melanoma are treated separately, following principles from cutaneous melanoma guidelines. The NCCN panel emphasizes clinical trials, smoking cessation, distress assessment, and consideration of HIV and HPV status (VULVA-1). Treatment philosophy balances curative intent with organ preservation and quality of life. For early-stage disease, conservative surgery with sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is preferred to reduce morbidity. Locally advanced disease is managed with chemoradiation, potentially followed by resection. Metastatic disease focuses on systemic therapy and palliative RT (VULVA-7).

by_intent.patient_group: Early-stage (Stage I and select Stage II) SCC/adenocarcinoma; resectable cutaneous vulvar melanoma stages 0-II; resectable mucosal vulvovaginal melanoma
by_intent.approach: Primary surgical resection with inguinofemoral lymph node evaluation (SLNB or lymphadenectomy) for SCC; partial vulvectomy with or without SLNB for melanoma. Adjuvant RT or chemoradiation based on risk factors (positive margins, LVSI, nodal metastases).

by_intent.patient_group: Locally advanced SCC/adenocarcinoma (unresectable without removing proximal urethra/bladder/anus)
by_intent.approach: Definitive chemoradiation with EBRT plus concurrent cisplatin-based chemotherapy; if complete response, surveillance; if residual disease, consider resection or additional therapy (VULVA-5, VULVA-6).

by_intent.patient_group: Metastatic disease beyond pelvis (Stage IVB); unresectable or recurrent vulvovaginal melanoma
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy (cisplatin/paclitaxel-based, immunotherapy, or targeted agents) and/or EBRT for locoregional control/symptom palliation; best supportive care as appropriate (VULVA-7, VM-1A/VM-2).

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Management should involve a multidisciplinary team including gynecologic oncology, radiation oncology, medical oncology, pathology, radiology, and supportive/palliative care. For patients with vulvar cancer who are older, the NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology should be consulted (VULVA-1). Smoking cessation counseling and intervention are recommended. Distress screening using the NCCN Distress Thermometer is required at all stages.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status (PS) is not explicitly defined but treatment decisions consider surgical candidacy, tolerance of chemoradiation, and ability to undergo lymphadenectomy. For older patients (≥65 years), geriatric assessment is advised (VULVA-1). For patients with unresectable disease, PS guides between curative chemoradiation and palliative approaches.

management_pathways.title: Early Stage SCC/adenocarcinoma – Lateral Lesions (Stage IA, IB, select Stage II)
branching_basis: Tumor location relative to midline (≥2 cm from vulvar midline for lateral)
Depth of invasion (≤1 mm vs >1 mm)

nodes.criteria: Stage IA (≤1 mm invasion), lateral lesion

options.label: Simple partial vulvectomy
options.recommendation: Observe if negative margins; if positive margins, proceed to VULVA-3 (adjuvant based on primary tumor risk factors). Lymph node evaluation not required (VULVA-2).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observation alone for negative margins; no nodal evaluation needed.

nodes.criteria: Stage IB (>1 mm invasion) or select Stage II, lateral lesion

options.label: Radical partial vulvectomy and ipsilateral inguinofemoral LN evaluation (SLNB or lymphadenectomy)
options.recommendation: Ipsilateral SLNB (preferred) or ipsilateral inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy. If SLN not detected, perform complete ipsilateral lymphadenectomy. Adjuvant therapy based on primary tumor risk factors (VULVA-3) and nodal evaluation (VULVA-4) (VULVA-2).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Based on primary tumor risk factors (close margins, LVSI, depth, pattern of invasion) and nodal findings (size of metastasis, number of positive nodes, ENE). See VULVA-3 and VULVA-4.

management_pathways.title: Early Stage SCC/adenocarcinoma – Near Midline or Midline Lesions (Stage IB, select Stage II)
branching_basis: Tumor location (anterior or posterior near midline/midline)

nodes.criteria: Stage IB (>1 mm invasion) or select Stage II, near midline or midline lesion (anterior or posterior)

options.label: Radical partial vulvectomy and bilateral inguinofemoral LN evaluation (SLNB or bilateral lymphadenectomy)
options.recommendation: Bilateral SLNB (preferred) or bilateral inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy. If SLN not detected on a side, perform ipsilateral complete lymphadenectomy on that side. Adjuvant therapy per primary tumor risk factors and nodal evaluation (VULVA-2, VULVA-3, VULVA-4).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Based on primary tumor risk factors and nodal pathology; see VULVA-3 and VULVA-4. For lateralized/near midline tumors with unilateral SLN metastasis, unilateral groin treatment acceptable. For midline tumors with unilateral SLN metastasis, unilateral groin treatment if contralateral groin negative.

management_pathways.title: Adjuvant Therapy Based on Primary Tumor Risk Factors (SCC/adenocarcinoma)
branching_basis: Other risk factors (close margins, LVSI, tumor size, depth of invasion, pattern of invasion)

nodes.criteria: Negative margins for invasive disease

options.label: Observe or Adjuvant EBRT based on other risk factors
options.preference: Observation is standard if no risk factors; EBRT considered if risk factors present
options.recommendation: Other primary risk factors include close tumor margins, LVSI, tumor size, depth of invasion, and pattern of invasion (spray or diffuse). Nodal involvement may also impact decision (VULVA-3).

adjuvant_logic.summary: If no risk factors, observe. If risk factors present, consider adjuvant EBRT to primary site.

nodes.criteria: Positive margins for invasive disease, resectable

options.label: Re-excision (preferred) or Adjuvant EBRT
options.recommendation: If re-excision yields negative margins, observe or consider EBRT based on risk factors. If margins remain positive after re-excision, give adjuvant EBRT (VULVA-3).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Re-excision first; if successful, manage per negative margin pathway. If not, EBRT. For positive margins of HSIL (noninvasive), individualize.

nodes.criteria: Positive margins for invasive disease, unresectable (without removing proximal urethra/bladder/anus)

options.label: Adjuvant EBRT
options.recommendation: EBRT to primary site. Consider concurrent chemotherapy based on nodal status and other factors (VULVA-3).

adjuvant_logic.summary: EBRT alone or with chemotherapy; if later becomes resectable, consider surgery.

management_pathways.title: Adjuvant Therapy Based on Nodal Evaluation (SCC/adenocarcinoma)
branching_basis: SLN status (negative, positive ≤2 mm, positive >2 mm)
Inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy result (negative, positive with number of nodes and ENE)

nodes.criteria: LNs negative (SLN(s) or inguinofemoral nodes)

options.label: Observe
options.recommendation: No adjuvant therapy required (VULVA-4).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observation alone.

nodes.criteria: Single positive SLN ≤2 mm metastasis; no completion lymphadenectomy

options.label: EBRT ± concurrent chemotherapy
options.preference: Recommended (category 2A, but note: RT alone 50 Gy is inferior to lymphadenectomy for >2 mm; for ≤2 mm, RT is standard based on GROINSS-V II)
options.recommendation: For SLN micrometastases (≤2 mm), EBRT alone is acceptable; consider adding chemotherapy or dose escalation to 54-60 Gy if concerns (VULVA-4).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT (with or without chemo) to groin/pelvis. If only one positive SLN ≤2 mm, RT alone can be considered; GROINSS-V II showed 2-year isolated groin recurrence 1.6% with RT for micrometastases (Ref 72).

nodes.criteria: Positive SLN >2 mm metastasis; or completion lymphadenectomy with ≥2 positive LNs or ENE

options.label: Complete inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy (preferred) then EBRT ± concurrent chemotherapy
options.preference: Lymphadenectomy preferred (category 1 for RT if ≥2 LNs positive or ENE)
options.recommendation: If patient not suitable for complete lymphadenectomy, EBRT ± chemo alone (selected). EBRT dose may be escalated to 54-60 Gy for >2 mm nodal involvement (VULVA-4).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Chemoradiation is strongly recommended for ≥2 positive LNs or ENE. For macrometastases >2 mm, GROINSS-V II showed 22% isolated groin recurrence with RT alone at 2 years, vs 6.9% with IFL; thus IFL preferred (Ref 72).

nodes.criteria: Inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy with positive LN(s) (any number)

options.label: EBRT ± concurrent chemotherapy (category 1 for RT if ≥2 LNs positive or ENE)
options.preference: Adjuvant RT recommended; concurrent chemotherapy often added
options.recommendation: Consider PET imaging to help decision-making. For single positive LN without ECE, factors favoring adjuvant RT: larger primary size, larger LN size, inadequate dissection, LN ratio >20%, LVSI, radiographically suspicious findings (VULVA-D 2 of 5).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT to groin/pelvis with or without chemo. For single positive LN, individualize; for ≥2 or ENE, RT is category 1.

management_pathways.title: Locally Advanced SCC/adenocarcinoma (Unresectable without removing proximal urethra/bladder/anus)

nodes.criteria: Radiographically negative nodes, no IFLN performed

options.label: EBRT + concurrent chemotherapy to primary tumor (± selective inguinofemoral LN coverage ± selective pelvic LN coverage)
options.recommendation: If IFLN performed and negative, treat primary only with selective LN coverage. If IFLN positive or not done, cover primary + groin/pelvis (VULVA-5).

adjuvant_logic.summary: After chemoradiation, evaluate response at ≥3 months (VULVA-6). If clinically negative, consider biopsy. If positive or residual, resect if operable or additional EBRT/systemic therapy.

nodes.criteria: Radiographically suspicious nodes (includes pelvic-confined M1 LN disease) – IFLN not performed

options.type: Consider FNA for enlarged LN, then Chemoradiation
options.label: EBRT + concurrent chemotherapy to primary tumor/inguinofemoral LNs/pelvic nodes
options.recommendation: Consider FNA to confirm nodal metastasis. Treatment fields cover all involved regions (VULVA-5).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Same response assessment as above. If suspicious nodes were not confirmed, consider additional imaging.

management_pathways.title: Metastatic Disease Beyond Pelvis (Stage IVB) – SCC/adenocarcinoma

nodes.criteria: Stage IVB with distant metastases

options.label: EBRT for locoregional control/symptom palliation and/or Systemic therapy
options.recommendation: Consider ablative therapy for 1-5 metastatic lesions if primary controlled (Ref Palma 2019). Best supportive care options (VULVA-7).

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy; treatment is palliative.

management_pathways.title: Vulva-Confined Recurrence, Clinically Negative Nodes, Not Previously Irradiated (SCC/adenocarcinoma)

nodes.criteria: Resectable, margins negative, nodes negative (surgically or clinically/radiographically)

options.label: Partial or total radical vulvectomy ± unilateral or bilateral inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy
options.recommendation: If nodes negative, observe; if nodes positive, proceed to adjuvant RT/chemo per VULVA-9 table (VULVA-9).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Margin negative, LN negative: Observe or EBRT. Margin positive, LN negative: Re-excision or EBRT ± brachytherapy ± chemo. Margin negative, LN surgically positive: EBRT ± concurrent chemo.

nodes.criteria: Resectable, margins positive, nodes negative (surgically or clinically/radiographically)

options.label: Re-excision or EBRT ± brachytherapy ± concurrent chemotherapy (category 2B for chemo)
options.recommendation: If re-excision yields negative margins, manage per negative margin pathway. If positive margins persist, EBRT ± chemo (VULVA-9).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Post re-excision: if margins negative, observe or EBRT; if margins still positive, EBRT ± brachytherapy ± chemo.

management_pathways.title: Vulva-Confined Recurrence, Nodes Clinically Negative, Previously Irradiated (SCC/adenocarcinoma)

nodes.criteria: Resectable

options.label: Resection (partial or total radical vulvectomy)
options.recommendation: Assess for resectability; if resectable, perform resection. No re-irradiation if prior full dose (VULVA-9).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT not typically feasible due to prior RT. Close surveillance.

nodes.criteria: Unresectable

options.type: Systemic therapy or Best supportive care
options.label: Systemic therapy or Best supportive care
options.preference: Consider systemic therapy first if appropriate
options.recommendation: Options include immunotherapy, chemotherapy (as per second-line), or palliative care (VULVA-9).

adjuvant_logic.summary: No further surgery; palliative management.

management_pathways.title: Confirmed Nodal or Distant Recurrence (SCC/adenocarcinoma)
branching_basis: Isolated inguinofemoral/pelvic LN vs distant metastasis

nodes.criteria: Isolated inguinofemoral/pelvic LN recurrence, no prior EBRT

options.label: EBRT + concurrent chemotherapy; consider resection of clinically enlarged LNs
options.recommendation: If no prior RT, give EBRT + chemo. Consider resection of suspicious LNs (VULVA-10).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Definitive treatment; surveillance after completion.

nodes.criteria: Isolated inguinofemoral/pelvic LN recurrence, prior EBRT

options.label: Systemic therapy and/or selective EBRT if feasible; consider resection
options.preference: Systemic therapy preferred; selective RT if small volume
options.recommendation: If limited, consider resection followed by systemic therapy. Otherwise, palliative systemic therapy (VULVA-10).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative intent.

nodes.criteria: Distant metastasis (with or without prior pelvic EBRT)

options.label: Systemic therapy and/or selective EBRT if feasible; consider ablative therapy for 1-5 lesions if primary controlled
options.recommendation: Best supportive care if not candidate (VULVA-10).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative.

nodes.criteria: Stage 0 or IA (resectable)

options.label: Partial vulvectomy (wide local excision with clear margins)
options.recommendation: Topical imiquimod can be considered for MIS if negative margins not feasible surgically (VM-1, VM-1A). Adjuvant: None for stage 0/IA; observation.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observation.

nodes.criteria: Stage IB (resectable)

options.label: Partial vulvectomy ± SLNB
options.recommendation: If SLNB negative, observation. If positive, proceed to adjuvant treatment per Melanoma Cutaneous guidelines (adjuvant nivolumab or pembrolizumab for stage IIB/C) (VM-1).

adjuvant_logic.summary: For stage IB with positive SLNB, manage as stage III per melanoma guidelines; for negative SLNB, observation.

nodes.criteria: Stage II (resectable)

options.label: Partial vulvectomy + SLNB
options.recommendation: If SLNB negative, consider adjuvant pembrolizumab (category 1) or nivolumab (category 1) for stage IIB/IIC per melanoma guidelines. If SLNB positive, proceed to adjuvant treatment for stage III (VM-1).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant checkpoint inhibitor for high-risk node-negative stage IIB/IIC; for node-positive, use stage III adjuvant options (nivolumab, pembrolizumab, or dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600+).

nodes.criteria: Stage III (resectable)

options.label: Partial vulvectomy with clear margins; consider neoadjuvant pembrolizumab or nivolumab/ipilimumab
options.recommendation: Post resection, based on pathologic response, consider adjuvant nivolumab (category 1), pembrolizumab (category 1), or dabrafenib/trametinib if BRAF V600+ (VM-1).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant systemic therapy per melanoma guidelines.

nodes.criteria: Unresectable or residual disease

options.label: Systemic therapy (anti-PD-1, combination checkpoint, targeted if BRAF+) ± RT
options.recommendation: RT can be considered for symptomatic local control. Follow melanoma cutaneous guidelines for systemic options (VM-1).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative systemic therapy ± RT.

nodes.criteria: Resectable, clear margins (at least 1 cm for vaginal lesions if feasible)

options.label: Wide local excision (at least 1 cm margin) and consider SLNB
options.recommendation: Observation ± brachytherapy (preferred for vaginal lesions) ± EBRT ± systemic therapy (VM-2).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Adjuvant RT (brachytherapy or EBRT) if close/positive margins or nodal concerns; systemic therapy considered based on high-risk features.

nodes.criteria: Unresectable or residual disease after surgery

options.label: Systemic therapy ± RT (EBRT and/or brachytherapy)
options.recommendation: Systemic therapy per melanoma cutaneous guidelines; RT for local control if symptomatic (VM-2).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative.

management_pathways.title: Biomarker-Driven Systemic Therapy for Advanced or Recurrent/Metastatic Vulvar Cancer (SCC/adenocarcinoma) – Second-Line or Subsequent

nodes.criteria: TMB-H tumors (≥10 mut/Mb) after prior treatment, no satisfactory alternatives

options.label: Pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Recommended for TMB-H tumors based on KEYNOTE-158. ORR for TMB-H vulvar cancer was ~17% (Ref 174).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Second-line or subsequent.

nodes.criteria: MSI-H/dMMR tumors after prior treatment

options.label: Pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Based on KEYNOTE-158; overall cohort ORR 34.3% (Ref 175).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Second-line or subsequent.

nodes.criteria: PD-L1 positive (CPS ≥1) disease progression on or after chemotherapy

options.label: Pembrolizumab
options.recommendation: Based on KEYNOTE-158 vulvar SCC cohort: ORR 10.9% overall, 9.5% in PD-L1 positive, median OS 6.2 months (Ref 173).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Second-line or subsequent.

nodes.criteria: HER2-positive tumors (IHC 3+ or 2+)

options.label: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki
options.recommendation: Based on DESTINY-PanTumor02; in cervical cancer cohort ORR 50%, for IHC3+ 75% (Ref 178). HER2 IHC testing recommended for advanced disease (VULVA-A 2 of 4).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Second-line or subsequent.

nodes.criteria: HPV-related tumor

options.label: Nivolumab
options.recommendation: Based on CheckMate 358; 12-month OS 40% for vaginal/vulvar cohort (Ref 179).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Second-line or subsequent.

nodes.criteria: NTRK gene fusion-positive tumors (naïve to prior NTRK therapy or progressed on prior therapy)

options.label: Larotrectinib, Entrectinib, or Repotrectinib
options.recommendation: ORR for larotrectinib 79% (Ref 183), entrectinib 61% (Ref 180), repotrectinib 58% (Ref 184).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Second-line or subsequent.

management_pathways.title: Surveillance for Vulvar and Vulvovaginal Melanoma

nodes.criteria: Stage ≥ IB

options.label: Groin nodal ultrasound every 3-6 months for 2 years, then 6-12 months for years 3-5; consider CT scan or FDG-PET/CT every 3-6 months for high-risk disease
options.recommendation: Nodal ultrasound requires specific radiologic expertise (VM-3).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Surveillance only; treat recurrence per melanoma guidelines.

items.text: History and physical examination (H&P) including vulvar, vaginal, cervical evaluation
Complete blood count (CBC), liver function tests/renal function studies
Biopsy of all suspicious lesions with pathologic review; histologic type (HPV-associated vs HPV-independent) and depth of invasion for SCC; melanoma in situ vs invasive
items.recommendation: Essential for diagnosis. For SCC, recommend p16 IHC to determine HPV status, p53 IHC for HPV-negative tumors (VULVA-A 1 of 4, VULVA-A 2 of 4). For melanoma, consider mutational testing (BRAF, KIT) (VM-1A).

items.text: Imaging: Consider pelvis MRI for treatment planning; consider neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis/groin FDG-PET/CT or chest/abdomen/pelvis CT for clinical stage II or higher or if metastasis suspected; chest x-ray initially; other imaging based on symptoms
items.recommendation: Imaging as needed for delineating extent of tumor or for treatment planning (VULVA-B, VULVA-1). For melanoma, see Principles of Imaging (ME-D) in Melanoma: Cutaneous (VM-1A).

items.text: Examination under anesthesia (EUA) cystoscopy or proctoscopy as indicated
items.recommendation: For SCC/adenocarcinoma and melanoma if involvement of bladder/rectum suspected (VULVA-1, VM-1).

items.text: Consider cervical HPV and cytology testing
items.recommendation: Due to multifocal nature of HPV-related lower genital tract neoplasia (VULVA-1).

items.text: Consider HIV testing, especially in younger patients with SCC or HPV-related disease
items.recommendation: If positive, refer to HIV specialist; do not modify cancer treatment based solely on HIV status (VULVA-1).

items.text: Smoking cessation counseling and intervention if indicated
items.recommendation: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Smoking Cessation (VULVA-1).

items.text: Assess for distress using NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List (includes social determinants of health)
items.recommendation: At all stages and settings (VULVA-1, VM-1, VM-2).

items.text: For patients ≥65 years, consult NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology
For melanoma: Full skin assessment for primary cutaneous lesions
Consider somatic mutational testing for BRAF, KIT for vulvar/vulvovaginal melanoma
items.recommendation: As clinically indicated to guide targeted therapy (VM-1A).

items.text: Imaging: neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis/groin FDG-PET/CT if not previously performed during surveillance; consider pelvis MRI for treatment planning
items.recommendation: Based on symptomatology or clinical concern (VULVA-B).

items.text: Biopsy to confirm local and/or distant recurrence
items.recommendation: Consider to confirm recurrence; core biopsy or FNA directed at suspicious nodes (VULVA-8, VM-3).

items.text: Laboratory: CBC, BUN, creatinine as indicated

surgery.role: Surgery is the primary treatment for early-stage vulvar cancer (SCC/adenocarcinoma and melanoma) and is used for resection of residual disease after chemoradiation in locally advanced cases, as well as for recurrence. It provides critical staging information via lymph node evaluation, which is the most important determinant of survival (Burger et al., Ref 33). The goal is complete tumor resection with negative margins while preserving function and quality of life where possible.
principles: Tumor margin status is a significant prognostic factor for local recurrence. Efforts should be made to obtain gross surgical margins of at least 1 cm at primary surgery. Studies question the traditional 8-mm pathologic free margin; a smaller margin may be acceptable, particularly to preserve sensitive areas and sexual function (VULVA-C 1 of 6). Close margins (1-8 mm) may be observed with regular follow-up; re-excision for positive margins should be considered. Adjuvant local RT is an alternative. Positive margins involving urethra, anus, or vagina may be unresectable without significant morbidity.
For invasive SCC, re-excision of close or positive margins may not be beneficial in patients with inguinal node metastases that require EBRT ± chemotherapy postoperatively (VULVA-C 1 of 6).

Lymph node status is the most important determinant of survival. Staging includes complete surgical resection of primary tumor with at least 1 cm clinical gross margins and either unilateral or bilateral inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy or SLNB in select patients (VULVA-C 2 of 6).
Historically, en bloc resection was performed; current standard involves separate incisions for vulvar tumor and LN resection to reduce morbidity (DiSaia et al., Ref 63).

SLNB is an alternative standard-of-care approach in select patients with SCC (tumor <4 cm, unifocal, clinically negative groin). It results in decreased postoperative morbidity without compromising detection of LN metastases. Dual tracer (radiocolloid + blue dye or ICG) improves sensitivity. SLNB should be performed by high-volume surgeons (VULVA-C 4 of 6).

If SLN is not detected on a side, complete inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy is recommended. For SLN metastases >2 mm, completion lymphadenectomy is preferred. For micrometastases (≤2 mm), RT alone is acceptable if lymphadenectomy not performed (GROINSS-V II, Ref 72).

For lateralized and near-midline tumors with unilateral SLN metastasis, unilateral groin treatment by either lymphadenectomy or RT is acceptable. For midline tumors with unilateral SLN metastasis, unilateral groin treatment is acceptable if contralateral groin negative (VULVA-C 4 of 6, Coleman et al., Ref 61).
Pelvic exenteration may be considered for select cases with central recurrence (VULVA-9).

procedures.name: Simple partial vulvectomy
procedures.approach: Resection of a portion of the vulva to superficial depth (skin and subcutaneous tissue).
procedures.indications: Stage IA SCC (≤1 mm invasion) (VULVA-2). Also for melanoma in situ if not requiring radical depth.
procedures.key_complications: Wound breakdown, infection, scarring, sexual dysfunction.

procedures.name: Radical partial vulvectomy
procedures.approach: Resection of a portion of the vulva to the urogenital diaphragm, median perineal fascia, or periosteum of pubic bone. Includes at least 1 cm clinical margins.
procedures.indications: Stage IB or II SCC; vulvar-confined recurrence; resectable melanoma with clear margins.
procedures.key_complications: Bleeding, wound infection, lymphedema (if combined with lymphadenectomy), sexual dysfunction, urinary dysfunction.

procedures.name: Radical total vulvectomy
procedures.approach: Complete removal of the vulva to deep fascia/periosteum.
procedures.indications: Extensive or multifocal disease; select recurrences (VULVA-9). Retrospective data show no difference in recurrence vs radical partial vulvectomy (VULVA-C 2 of 6).
procedures.key_complications: Similar to radical partial but higher wound complication rate, vaginal stenosis, psychosexual impact.

procedures.name: Inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy (IFLN)
procedures.approach: Removal of lymph nodes along inguinal ligament, within proximal femoral triangle, and deep to cribriform fascia. Separate incision from vulvar resection.
procedures.indications: For patients not meeting SLNB criteria (tumor ≥4 cm, multifocal, clinically positive nodes, prior vulvar surgery). Also as completion after positive SLN >2 mm or when SLN not detected.
procedures.key_complications: Wound complications (20-40%), lymphedema (30-70%), lymphocele, cellulitis, nerve injury (VULVA-C 2 of 6, Carlson et al., Ref 62).

procedures.name: Sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB)
procedures.approach: Peritumor injection of radiocolloid (99mTc) and blue dye (Isosulfan Blue 1%) or ICG; gamma probe detection; ultrastaging of SLN.
procedures.indications: SCC: unifocal tumor <4 cm, clinically negative groin nodes, no previous vulvar surgery. Melanoma: stage IB and above with clinically negative nodes (VULVA-C 4 of 6).
procedures.key_complications: Lower morbidity than full IFLN; allergic reaction to blue dye; false-negative rate ~3.7% overall (2% for tumors <4 cm) (GOG 173, Ref 66).

procedures.name: Pelvic exenteration
procedures.approach: Removal of pelvic organs (bladder, rectum, and/or reproductive organs) en bloc with vulvar resection.
procedures.indications: Select cases of central recurrence not amenable to less radical surgery, after chemoradiation failure (VULVA-6, VULVA-9).
procedures.key_complications: High morbidity: stoma formation, infection, fistula, sexual dysfunction, thrombosis.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is defined as ability to remove all gross disease without removing proximal urethra, bladder, or anus (VULVA-5). For locally advanced disease, unresectability is defined as tumor fixed to these structures that would require exenteration. After chemoradiation, reassess resectability; if residual disease is considered operable, proceed with resection (VULVA-6). For recurrence, resectability depends on prior RT history, location, and involvement of critical structures (VULVA-9).

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy is used as adjuvant therapy following initial surgery for SCC/adenocarcinoma (e.g., for positive margins, nodal metastases), as primary therapy (with concurrent chemotherapy) for locally advanced disease, and for palliation in recurrent/metastatic disease. For melanoma, RT is considered for medically inoperable patients, adjuvant in high-risk settings, and for symptomatic control.
principles: Radiation technique and doses are critical to maximize tumor control while limiting toxicity. CT-based treatment planning with conformal blocking is standard. IMRT is recommended for better dose delivery to target and sparing of organs at risk; 2D/3D technique reserved for urgent/palliative cases (VULVA-D 1 of 5).

Target volumes should be defined by physical exam and CT simulation; radio-opaque markers on key landmarks (anus, urethra, clitoris, scars) aid planning. MRI and PET fusion help delineate gross disease. Consensus contouring guidelines (Gaffney et al., Ref 3) are recommended.
Bolus should be used to ensure adequate dose to superficial targets (vulvar and groin). Dose verification with TLD/OSLD is recommended (VULVA-D 3 of 5).

Acute effects (diarrhea, bladder irritation, fatigue, mucocutaneous reaction) are expected; aggressive management is needed. Treatment breaks should be avoided. Antifungal treatment for Candida overgrowth can reduce skin reaction. Postoperative adjuvant RT should start within 6-8 weeks after adequate healing (VULVA-D 1 of 5).
For locally advanced disease, concurrent chemotherapy (cisplatin preferred) is standard based on improved survival vs RT alone (NCDB analysis: 5-year OS 49.9% vs 27.4%, HR 0.76, Ref 108).

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant to primary surgical bed (negative margins)
Adjuvant to primary surgical bed (close or positive margins)
dose_frameworks.indication: Close/positive margins after maximal resection (VULVA-D 4 of 5).

dose_frameworks.name: Positive inguinofemoral LNs without ECE or gross residual
dose_frameworks.indication: Postoperative positive nodes without extranodal extension (VULVA-D 4 of 5).

dose_frameworks.name: Extranodal extension (ECE) in nodal disease
dose_frameworks.indication: Postoperative with ECE or unresectable gross nodes (VULVA-D 4 of 5).

dose_frameworks.name: Gross primary vulvar disease (definitive or boost)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Once daily, 5 days/week. Consider boost to 70 Gy for bulky/persistent disease.
dose_frameworks.indication: Definitive chemoradiation for unresectable primary; boost to residual disease (VULVA-D 4 of 5).

dose_frameworks.name: Melanoma – Definitive (vulvar) - PTV high risk
