dose_frameworks.dose_per_fraction: Various (e.g., 66 Gy in 2.2 Gy/fx or 70 Gy in 2 Gy/fx)
dose_frameworks.schedule: Once daily; more hypofractionated regimens for small volumes.
dose_frameworks.indication: Primary RT for unresectable vulvar melanoma (VM-A 1 of 7).

dose_frameworks.name: Melanoma – Adjuvant (vulvar or vaginal)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 45-65 Gy (lower range for elective, higher for positive margins/ECE)
dose_frameworks.indication: Postoperative for close/positive margins or high-risk nodal disease (VM-A 1 of 7, VM-A 5 of 7).

dose_frameworks.name: Vaginal Melanoma – Definitive (EBRT + brachytherapy boost)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: EBRT 44-50 Gy (2 Gy/fx) or 54-63 Gy (1.8 Gy/fx) plus brachytherapy boost (e.g., 4.5-6 Gy x5, 7 Gy x4, 8-9 Gy x3)
dose_frameworks.schedule: EBRT once daily; brachytherapy as per institutional protocol.

approaches.name: Adjuvant EBRT (SCC/adenocarcinoma)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45-50.4 Gy/25-28 fractions (1.8 Gy) for negative margins; 54-60 Gy for close/positive margins; 50-64 Gy for nodes depending on ECE
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Yes, often with cisplatin (preferred) or carboplatin if intolerant; other options: cisplatin/fluorouracil, cisplatin/gemcitabine, capecitabine/mitomycin, gemcitabine, paclitaxel (category 2A/B). Concurrent chemo recommended for primary site risk factors? Usually considered for nodal positivity; for primary site alone, RT alone may suffice (VULVA-E, VULVA-D).
approaches.indication: Postoperative for positive margins, LVSI, deep invasion, or nodal metastases. For ≥2 positive LNs or ENE, RT is category 1 (VULVA-4).
approaches.key_trial: GOG 37 showed survival benefit for adjuvant RT vs pelvic LN resection in node-positive patients; long-term follow-up: disease-related death 51% vs 29%, HR 0.49 (Ref 125). AGO-CaRE-1: 3-year PFS 39.6% vs 25.9% (P=0.004) for RT vs no RT in node-positive (Ref 128).
approaches.toxicities: Acute skin reactions, diarrhea, cystitis, fatigue. Late effects: fibrosis, vulvovaginal atrophy, stenosis, bone loss, pelvic fractures, secondary malignancy risk.

approaches.name: Definitive Chemoradiation (Locally Advanced SCC/adenocarcinoma)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 59.4-64.8 Gy in 33-36 fractions (1.8 Gy) with boost to 70 Gy for bulky disease
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Preferred: cisplatin 40 mg/m2 weekly (or carboplatin AUC 2 if intolerant). Other: cisplatin/fluorouracil, cisplatin/gemcitabine (category 2A).
approaches.indication: Unresectable SCC/adenocarcinoma of vulva without removing proximal urethra/bladder/anus (VULVA-5).
approaches.key_trial: GOG 205: pCR 78% in patients undergoing biopsy after chemoradiation with weekly cisplatin (Ref 110). GOG 101: 96% avoided pelvic exenteration (Ref 99). NCDB analysis: chemoradiation improved 5-year OS vs RT alone (49.9% vs 27.4%, HR 0.76, Ref 108).
approaches.toxicities: Grade ≥3 skin/mucosa reactions, pain, diarrhea, hematologic toxicity, late fibrosis, fistulae (less common).

approaches.name: Palliative RT (SCC/adenocarcinoma or Melanoma)
approaches.dose_fractionation: Variable: e.g., 30 Gy/10 fractions, 48-50 Gy/20 fractions, or hypofractionated 6 Gy x5 for small fields.
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Not routinely used; chemo may be given sequentially.
approaches.indication: Symptomatic locoregional or metastatic disease (VULVA-7, VM-A 6 of 7).
approaches.key_trial: SABR-COMET: SABR improved OS in oligometastatic disease (median OS 41 vs 28 months, Ref 150).
approaches.toxicities: Palliation aims to minimize toxicity; depends on site and dose.

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy for vulvar SCC is largely extrapolated from cervical cancer due to limited vulvar-specific data. Chemoradiation regimens use platinum-based radiosensitizers. For advanced, recurrent, or metastatic disease, first-line therapy includes platinum/paclitaxel with or without bevacizumab and/or pembrolizumab. Second-line options include checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, cemiplimab, nivolumab, ipilimumab+nivolumab) and biomarker-directed therapies (TMB-H, MSI-H, PD-L1, HER2, NTRK). For vulvar and vulvovaginal melanoma, systemic therapy follows the NCCN Guidelines for Melanoma: Cutaneous. Regimens are category 2A unless otherwise noted.

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Extrapolated from cervical cancer; weekly cisplatin is standard radiosensitizer with acceptable toxicity.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Carboplatin AUC 2 weekly if cisplatin intolerant; other recommended: cisplatin/gemcitabine, cisplatin/fluorouracil, capecitabine/mitomycin, gemcitabine, paclitaxel (all category 2A except as noted). Cisplatin/gemcitabine studied in NRG/GOG 279 with 73% pCR (Ref 157). (VULVA-E 1 of 2)

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-Line Therapy for Advanced or Recurrent/Metastatic SCC (Stage IVB or recurrence not amenable to curative therapy)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cisplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab ± Bevacizumab OR Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab ± Bevacizumab
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on KEYNOTE-826 in cervical cancer showing improved OS with pembrolizumab added to chemo (±bevacizumab). Regimens extrapolated.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cisplatin/Paclitaxel or Carboplatin/Paclitaxel alone; Cisplatin/Paclitaxel + Bevacizumab; Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Bevacizumab. Category 2A. Single-agent cisplatin or carboplatin also listed as 'other recommended'. (VULVA-E 1 of 2)

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Second-Line or Subsequent Therapy for Advanced or Recurrent/Metastatic SCC
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: No single preferred; options include paclitaxel, cemiplimab, erlotinib (category 2B), cisplatin/gemcitabine (category 2B), ipilimumab + nivolumab (new in v2.2026). Biomarker-directed options: pembrolizumab for TMB-H, MSI-H/dMMR, PD-L1 positive; fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan for HER2+; nivolumab for HPV-related; larotrectinib/entrectinib/repotrectinib for NTRK fusion.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on KEYNOTE-158 (pembrolizumab), Empower-Cervical-1 (cemiplimab), CheckMate 358/848 (nivolumab±ipilimumab), DESTINY-PanTumor02 (T-DXd), and NTRK inhibitor trials.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: See individual biomarker categories. Note: first-line agents can be reused as second-line if not previously given. (VULVA-E 1 of 2, VULVA-E 1A of 2)

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Systemic therapy for Metastatic or Unresectable Vulvar/Vulvovaginal Melanoma
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Melanoma: Cutaneous (MELSYS-1). Options include anti-PD-1 monotherapy (pembrolizumab, nivolumab), combination nivolumab/ipilimumab, and BRAF-targeted therapy if BRAF V600 mutation positive.
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Vulvar melanoma is rare; data from cutaneous melanoma are extrapolated. CheckMate 067, KEYNOTE-006, etc. Molecular testing for BRAF, KIT recommended (VM-1A).
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Consider clinical trials given rarity. RT may be combined for local control.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin (Chemoradiation)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nephrotoxicity, neurotoxicity, ototoxicity, myelosuppression, nausea, electrolyte disturbances.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (Chemoradiation)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Weekly during RT; NRG/GOG 279 used 5 weeks (Ref 157).
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, fatigue, skin toxicity, nausea. pCR 73% in phase II.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab ± Bevacizumab (First-Line Advanced/Metastatic)
key_regimens.setting: Advanced, recurrent, or metastatic SCC (preferred first-line)

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg (IV) or 400 mg (IV q6 weeks) or subcutaneous (berahyaluronidase alfa)
drugs.day: Day 1 every 3 weeks (IV) or every 6 weeks (400 mg IV); SC per product info

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neuropathy, myelosuppression, fatigue, hypertension (bevacizumab), immune-related adverse events (pembrolizumab: colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies).

key_regimens.name: Carboplatin/Paclitaxel + Pembrolizumab ± Bevacizumab (First-Line Advanced/Metastatic)
key_regimens.setting: Alternative first-line if cisplatin not tolerated

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg IV or 400 mg IV q6 weeks or SC

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression (more with carboplatin), neuropathy, alopecia, immune-related events.

key_regimens.name: Cemiplimab (Second-Line)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent for advanced/metastatic SCC
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, rash, diarrhea, immune-related adverse events. OS benefit in cervical cancer (Empower-Cervical-1: median OS 12 vs 8.5 months, HR 0.69; Ref 162).

key_regimens.name: Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (Second-Line)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent for advanced/metastatic SCC

drugs.name: Ipilimumab
drugs.day: Every 3 weeks for 4 doses (induction) then maintenance per protocol

key_regimens.cycle_days: 21-day cycles for 4 cycles then maintenance nivolumab 240 mg q2 weeks or 480 mg q4 weeks.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: High rate of immune-related adverse events (colitis, hepatitis, pneumonitis, rash). CheckMate 358 in cervical cancer and CheckMate 848 in TMB-H solid tumors (Ref 163, 164).

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab (Biomarker-Directed – MSI-H/dMMR, TMB-H, PD-L1+)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent for biomarker-selected patients

drugs.name: Pembrolizumab
drugs.dose: 200 mg (IV) or 400 mg (IV q6 weeks) or SC
drugs.day: Day 1 every 3 weeks (200 mg) or every 6 weeks (400 mg)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related events: thyroiditis, colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, myositis, etc. ORR 10.9% in PD-L1+ vulvar SCC (KEYNOTE-158, Ref 173); ORR 34.3% in MSI-H non-colorectal (Ref 175); ORR ~17% in TMB-H vulvar (Ref 174).

key_regimens.name: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (HER2+ Tumors)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent for advanced/metastatic SCC with HER2 IHC 3+ or 2+
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Interstitial lung disease, nausea, fatigue, myelosuppression. ORR 50% in cervical cancer cohort (Ref 178).

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab (HPV-Related Tumors)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent for HPV-related advanced/metastatic SCC

drugs.name: Nivolumab
drugs.dose: 240 mg (IV) or 480 mg (IV q4 weeks) or SC
drugs.day: Every 2 weeks (240 mg) or every 4 weeks (480 mg)

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related toxicities. CheckMate 358: 12-month OS 40% in vaginal/vulvar cohort (Ref 179).

key_regimens.name: NTRK Inhibitors (Larotrectinib, Entrectinib, Repotrectinib)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent for NTRK fusion-positive advanced/metastatic SCC or melanoma
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue, elevated transaminases, dizziness, constipation. ORR 79% (Ref 183).

key_regimens.name: Erlotinib (Category 2B)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line or subsequent for advanced/metastatic SCC (other recommended)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Rash, diarrhea, fatigue. Phase II: partial response 27.5%, stable disease 40% (Ref 122).

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Response after Definitive Chemoradiation for Locally Advanced Vulvar Cancer
treatment_response_assessment.timing: At least 3 months after completion of EBRT with concurrent chemotherapy (VULVA-6). For other settings (e.g., adjuvant therapy), response is assessed clinically and via imaging as part of surveillance (VULVA-8).
response_logic: Clinically negative for residual tumor at primary site and nodes: proceed to surveillance. Consider biopsy of tumor bed to confirm pathologic complete response (pCR). If biopsy negative, continue surveillance; if biopsy positive, manage as indicated below (VULVA-6).

Clinically suspicious for residual tumor at primary site and/or nodes: perform biopsy and imaging (consider FDG-PET/CT or MRI). If biopsy negative and margins negative, consider additional surgery, additional EBRT, and/or systemic therapy, or best supportive care. If biopsy positive and resectable, resect if deemed operable; if margins positive after resection, consider additional surgery, additional EBRT, systemic therapy, or best supportive care. If unresectable, consider additional EBRT and/or systemic therapy, or best supportive care (VULVA-6).

If resection of residual disease is performed and margins are negative, consider observation or additional treatment per risk factors. Pelvic exenteration may be considered for select cases (VULVA-6 footnote s).
imaging_recommendations: After definitive chemoradiation: consider FDG-PET/CT and/or pelvis MRI at 3-6 months to assess treatment response (VULVA-B).

During surveillance: imaging as indicated based on symptoms or examination findings suspicious for recurrence. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis/groin FDG-PET/CT if recurrence/metastasis suspected (VULVA-B).
For surveillance of vulvar/vulvovaginal melanoma: CT scan or FDG-PET/CT every 3-6 months, especially for high-risk disease (VM-3).
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: If after definitive chemoradiation there is clinically negative exam, biopsy may be considered to confirm pCR but is not mandatory (VULVA-6).

If clinically suspicious or biopsy positive for residual invasive disease, assess resectability. If resectable, proceed with surgery (radical partial or total vulvectomy ± inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy). If unresectable, offer additional EBRT (if feasible), systemic therapy, or best supportive care (VULVA-6).

For recurrence after prior treatment, see recurrence pathways (VULVA-9, VULVA-10). For isolated inguinofemoral/pelvic nodal recurrence not previously irradiated, consider resection of clinically enlarged LNs followed by EBRT + concurrent chemotherapy. For distant metastasis or prior EBRT, systemic therapy and/or selective EBRT, with consideration of ablative therapy for 1-5 lesions if primary controlled (VULVA-10, footnote t).

prognosis.overall: Vulvar cancer accounts for 5%–8% of gynecologic malignancies, with an estimated 7480 new diagnoses and 1770 deaths in the United States in 2025 [1-3]. The median age at diagnosis is 68 years [1-3]. Ninety percent of vulvar cancers are squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) [7]. Based on SEER data (2015–2021), 5-year overall survival (OS) for localized disease (stages I/II) is 85.7%, for regional or locally advanced disease (stages III/IVA) is 49.71%, and for stage IVB (which includes pelvic nodal disease) is 21.9% [4]. For vulvar melanoma, the 5-year OS is 47%, significantly worse than cutaneous melanoma (92%) [190]. Within SCC, HPV-associated tumors have a better prognosis than HPV-independent tumors [VULVA-A 1 of 4]. HPV-independent SCC is further subdivided by TP53 status: p53 abnormal SCCs have the worst clinical outcomes among the three molecular categories (HPV-positive, HPV-negative/p53 mutant, HPV-negative p53 wild-type) [6].

by_stage.context: OS for vulvar melanoma compared to cutaneous melanoma (92%) [190]

prognostic_factors: Lymph node (LN) status is the most important determinant of survival [14,33]
Number of positive LNs and presence of extracapsular extension (ECE) independently predict poorer survival [32,35-38]
Depth of invasion, tumor size, lymphovascular space invasion (LVSI), and surgical margin distance influence recurrence risk [19,32,39-44]
HPV-associated SCC has better prognosis than HPV-independent SCC [VULVA-A 1 of 4]
Within HPV-independent SCC, p53 aberrant (mutant) status portends the worst outcomes; p53 wild-type is intermediate [6]
For vulvar melanoma, molecular alterations (BRAF, NRAS, KIT) and tumor thickness affect prognosis [191-193]
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognostic factors guide the evolution from radical en bloc surgery to more conservative, individualized treatment for early-stage disease. LN metastasis remains the pivotal determinant for adjuvant therapy decisions. The molecular subclassification of vulvar SCC (HPV/p53 status) is increasingly recognized and recommended for pathologic reporting.

disease_related.complication: Pruritus, pain/irritation, vulvar bleeding or discharge
disease_related.management: Symptom-directed care; biopsy for diagnosis; surgical excision or chemoradiation for definitive treatment

disease_related.complication: Multifocal lesions (HPV-associated) and concurrent cervical neoplasia
disease_related.management: Full lower genital tract evaluation including cervical cytology/HPV testing

chemotherapy: Neurotoxicity, cardiac toxicity, cognitive dysfunction, secondary hematologic cancers [VULVA-F]
Chemotherapy-induced nausea/vomiting, myelosuppression are anticipated but specific antiemetic or growth factor protocols are not detailed in the guideline
immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (e.g., colitis, pneumonitis, endocrinopathies); management per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities [footnote e]
surgery: Lymphedema (30%–70% of patients after inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy) [62-64]
Adhesion formation, small bowel obstruction, urinary or gastrointestinal complications (e.g., incontinence, diarrhea), pelvic floor dysfunction, sexual dysfunction [VULVA-F]
Management includes physical therapy, pelvic floor therapy, lymphedema care, sexual health counseling
radiation: Acute effects: diarrhea, bladder irritation, fatigue, mucocutaneous reaction; accentuated by concurrent chemotherapy [VULVA-D 1 of 5]
Candida overgrowth (manage with oral/local antifungals); bacterial infection (prompt antibiotics) [VULVA-D 1 of 5]
Long-term: fibrosis, vulvovaginal atrophy, secondary cancers of skin/subcutaneous tissue/underlying organs, bone loss and pelvic fractures [VULVA-F]
Management: recommend bone density testing and prophylactic bisphosphonates for osteoporosis; vaginal dilators, moisturizers, local estrogen for vaginal health; smoking cessation

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care in vulvar cancer spans the entire disease trajectory and includes management of acute treatment toxicities, prevention and treatment of long-term effects, psychosocial support, and lifestyle interventions. The NCCN Guidelines provide dedicated sections on gynecologic survivorship (VULVA-F) and cross-reference the NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship, Distress Management, and Smoking Cessation.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: General healthy lifestyle and nutrition counseling are recommended for all survivors [VULVA-8, footnote w]. No specific nutritional protocols are detailed for vulvar cancer. Patients undergoing chemoradiation may require dietary modifications for mucositis or gastrointestinal symptoms.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Not specified in the NCCN Vulvar Cancer Guideline. Standard institutional antiemetic protocols for chemotherapy (e.g., cisplatin-based) should be followed; refer to NCCN Guidelines for Antiemesis.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Not specified. Use of granulocyte colony-stimulating factors (G-CSF) for febrile neutropenia prophylaxis is not discussed; standard practice per NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Growth Factors may be applied.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Not addressed specifically for vulvar cancer. Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis should follow standard perioperative and oncologic guidelines.
supportive_care.pain_management: For radiation-related mucocutaneous pain, aggressive local skin care and symptomatic medications are recommended [VULVA-D 1 of 5]. For chronic pain or neuropathic symptoms, referral to pain specialists may be indicated. Best supportive care (including palliative care) is an option for advanced/metastatic disease [VULVA-7].
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: All patients should be assessed for distress at every stage using the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health [VULVA-1, footnote d]. Psychosocial effects include depression, anxiety, fear of recurrence, altered body image, financial concerns, and interpersonal/sexual issues. Referral to appropriate providers (psychotherapy, sexual therapy, social work) is recommended [VULVA-F].
supportive_care.dental_care: Not specifically addressed. For patients receiving chemotherapy or radiation that may affect oral mucosa, standard dental evaluation and preventive care are advised.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After completion of primary curative treatment, all patients with vulvar cancer (SCC/adenocarcinoma and melanoma) should enter a structured follow-up program aimed at early detection of recurrence, management of treatment toxicities, and promotion of health-related quality of life. For SCC/adenocarcinoma, history and physical examination are recommended every 3–6 months for the first 2 years, then every 6–12 months for years 3–5, and annually thereafter (with more frequent visits for high-risk disease) [VULVA-8]. For vulvar melanoma (stage ≥IB), groin nodal ultrasound is recommended every 3–6 months for the first 2 years and every 6–12 months for years 3–5; CT scan and/or FDG-PET/CT may be considered every 3–6 months, especially for high-risk disease [VM-3]. Cervical/vaginal cytology with HPV testing is recommended as indicated for detection of lower genital tract neoplasia, though its value in detecting recurrent vulvar cancer is limited and accuracy is reduced after pelvic radiation [VULVA-8, footnotes u,v]. Imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or FDG-PET/CT) and laboratory assessment (CBC, BUN, creatinine) are recommended when symptoms or exam findings suggest recurrence [VULVA-8]. Patient education should cover symptoms of recurrence, vulvar dystrophy, periodic self-examination, lifestyle modifications (obesity, exercise, smoking cessation), and sexual health including vaginal dilators, lubricants, local estrogen, and hormone therapy for menopause [VULVA-8, footnote w].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Most recurrences of vulvar cancer occur within the first 1–2 years, but a significant subset occur beyond 5 years, justifying long-term follow-up [138,139]. Definitive data on optimal surveillance are lacking, but the NCCN Panel concurs with SGO recommendations [141]. LN status at initial diagnosis is the strongest predictor of recurrence risk; patients with positive nodes require closer surveillance. The goal is to detect potentially curable local/regional recurrences while avoiding unnecessary imaging in low-risk patients.
late_effects_screening: Bone density testing and prophylactic bisphosphonates for patients with osteoporosis or prior pelvic RT [VULVA-F]
Assessment for lymphedema (refer to lymphedema specialist if present) [VULVA-F]
Screening for secondary cancers (skin, subcutaneous tissue, organs in RT field) [VULVA-F]
Cardiovascular risk factor monitoring, recommended vaccinations, and general chronic disease management [VULVA-F]
Psychosocial screening: depression, anxiety, fear of recurrence, body image, sexual function, financial toxicity [VULVA-F]
For treatment-related menopause: consider hormone therapy [VULVA-F]
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: A multicenter case series of 502 patients reported that 37% developed recurrent SCC: 53.4% vulvar, 18.7% inguinal, 14.2% multi-site, 7.9% distant, 5.7% pelvic [34]. Survival at 5 years was 60% for vulvar recurrence, 27% for inguinal/pelvic, 15% for distant, and 14% for multiple sites [34]. Nodal recurrence carries a very poor prognosis regardless of treatment approach [139,144,146]. For vulvar melanoma, recurrence patterns follow those of cutaneous melanoma with locoregional (in-transit, nodal) and distant spread.

clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: H&P every 3–6 months for 2 years, then every 6–12 months for 3–5 years, then annually; adjust frequency based on risk of recurrence [VULVA-8]
Groin nodal ultrasound every 3–6 months for first 2 years, then every 6–12 months for years 3–5; consider CT or FDG-PET/CT every 3–6 months for high-risk disease [VM-3]

imaging_strategy: Initial post-treatment: Consider FDG-PET/CT and/or pelvis MRI at 3–6 months to assess treatment response after definitive primary treatment [VULVA-B]
During surveillance: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis or neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis/groin FDG-PET/CT if recurrence/metastasis is suspected based on symptoms or exam [VULVA-B]
For vulvar melanoma: FDG-PET/CT especially for high-risk disease every 3–6 months [VM-3]; groin nodal ultrasound requires specific radiologic expertise [VM-3, footnote m]
Imaging should be based on symptomatology and clinical concern; routine surveillance imaging in asymptomatic patients is not recommended
laboratory_monitoring: CBC, BUN, creatinine as indicated based on symptoms or examination findings suspicious for recurrence [VULVA-8]
No routine tumor markers are recommended for vulvar cancer
supportive_follow_up: Cervical/vaginal cytology and HPV testing as indicated for lower genital tract neoplasia detection (accuracy may be affected by prior pelvic RT) [VULVA-8]
Patient education on symptoms of recurrence, periodic self-exams, lifestyle (obesity, exercise), sexual health (dilators, lubricants, local estrogen), and smoking cessation [VULVA-8]
Referral to survivorship clinics, physical therapy, pelvic floor therapy, sexual therapy, psychotherapy as needed [VULVA-F]
Distress assessment at all disease stages using NCCN Distress Thermometer [VULVA-1]

special_situations.key_message: Cutaneous vulvar melanoma is defined as lesions outside Hart's line; mucosal vulvovaginal melanoma occurs inside Hart's line. Management follows principles from NCCN Guidelines for Melanoma: Cutaneous, adapted for anatomic location.
special_situations.management: Cutaneous: Partial vulvectomy for stage 0/IA; add SLNB for stage IB/II; for stage III consider neoadjuvant therapy or wide excision with SLNB and adjuvant per melanoma guidelines. Mucosal: Wide local excision with clear margins (≥1 cm for invasive melanoma) and consider SLNB; RT (brachytherapy ± EBRT) for close margins or unresectable disease; systemic therapy per melanoma guidelines.
special_situations.evidence: OS for vulvar melanoma is 47% vs 92% for cutaneous melanoma [190]. Molecular profile differs: lower BRAF mutations, higher KIT and PD-L1 alterations [191-193]. Refer to NCCN Melanoma: Cutaneous for detailed adjuvant and systemic therapy recommendations.

special_situations.key_message: Patients with vulvar cancer and HIV should be referred to an HIV specialist. Cancer treatment should not be modified solely on the basis of HIV status.
special_situations.management: Treat per standard vulvar cancer guidelines; consider HIV testing especially in younger patients or those with other HPV-related disease [VULVA-1]. See NCCN Guidelines for Cancer in People with HIV.
special_situations.evidence: NCCN Panel consensus; no randomized data specific to vulvar cancer in HIV population.

special_situations.key_message: Older patients may have comorbidities that affect treatment tolerability and should be evaluated using geriatric assessment principles.
special_situations.management: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology. Consider performance status, comorbid conditions, and patient preferences when selecting surgery, RT, or systemic therapy.
special_situations.evidence: NCCN Panel recommendation; vulvar cancer median age 68, with many patients ≥65 years [4].

special_situations.key_message: Routine assessment for distress using the NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List is recommended at all disease stages (including initial workup and follow-up) [VULVA-1, VM-1, VM-2].
special_situations.management: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management. Offer psychological support, social work, and financial counseling as needed. Social determinants of health should be addressed.
special_situations.evidence: NCCN Panel recommendation; distress is common in cancer patients and impacts quality of life and treatment adherence.

special_situations.key_message: Smoking is a risk factor for vulvar neoplasia, especially HPV-associated disease. All patients who smoke should be counseled to quit and offered cessation interventions.
special_situations.management: Refer to NCCN Guidelines for Smoking Cessation. Provide resources (e.g., smokefree.gov). Advise against restarting smoking after treatment.
special_situations.evidence: Cigarette smoking is a known risk factor for vulvar cancer [overview]. Smoking cessation improves overall health and may reduce recurrence risk.

clinical_pearls: Lymph node status remains the most powerful prognostic determinant in vulvar SCC; complete inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy or sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) is mandatory for all patients with stage IB or greater disease.

SLNB is an alternative to full lymphadenectomy for carefully selected patients: clinically/radiologically negative groins, unifocal primary tumor <4 cm, no prior vulvar surgery. Dual tracer (radiocolloid plus blue dye) is recommended for optimal detection.

HPV-associated and HPV-independent vulvar SCC follow distinct molecular pathways; p53 IHC is recommended to determine TP53 status in HPV-negative tumors, as p53-aberrant tumors have the worst prognosis.

For locally advanced vulvar cancer (unresectable without removing proximal urethra/bladder/anus), definitive chemoradiation is the preferred primary treatment. Biopsy at ≥3 months post-treatment can confirm pathologic complete response; if residual disease is resectable, surgery should be considered.

Adjuvant RT (category 1 for ≥2 positive LNs or extranodal extension) with or without concurrent chemotherapy improves overall survival in node-positive patients. Chemoradiation is particularly recommended for patients with >2 mm SLN metastasis or >1 positive node.
Groin recurrences carry a grave prognosis and are difficult to salvage; therefore, initial adequate surgical staging (SLNB or lymphadenectomy) and appropriate adjuvant therapy are critical.

Gynecologic survivorship care must address lymphedema, pelvic floor dysfunction, sexual health (dilators, lubricants, local estrogen), bone health (bone density testing after pelvic RT), and psychosocial distress. Long-term follow-up is needed because recurrences can occur beyond 5 years.

key_trials.full_name: GROningen INternational Study on Sentinel nodes in Vulvar cancer I
key_trials.population: Females with primary vulvar SCC <4 cm, clinically negative groins
key_trials.intervention: SLNB; if negative, no further groin surgery
key_trials.key_result: Groin recurrence in 2.3% (6/259) with unifocal tumor and negative SLN; 3-year survival rate 97%
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: SLN positivity rate 33%; DSS worse for SLN metastases >2 mm vs ≤2 mm (69.5% vs 94.4%, P=.001)
key_trials.practice_change: Established SLNB as safe and effective management for early-stage vulvar cancer with unifocal tumors <4 cm and negative SLN; reduced morbidity compared to full lymphadenectomy.

key_trials.full_name: GROningen INternational Study on Sentinel nodes in Vulvar cancer II
key_trials.population: Patients with positive SLN (160 micrometastases ≤2 mm, 162 macrometastases >2 mm)
key_trials.intervention: IF radiotherapy for patients with SLN micrometastases; IF lymphadenectomy or RT for macrometastases
key_trials.comparator: Observational comparison between RT and IF lymphadenectomy for macrometastases
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Isolated groin recurrence rate at 2 years
key_trials.key_result: Micrometastases: 2-year isolated groin recurrence 1.6% with RT. Macrometastases: 22% with RT vs 6.9% with IFL (P=.011)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Treatment-related morbidity lower with RT compared to IFL
key_trials.practice_change: For SLN micrometastases (≤2 mm), IF radiotherapy is a safe alternative to completion lymphadenectomy. For macrometastases, IF lymphadenectomy remains preferred, though RT is an option for patients unsuitable for surgery.

key_trials.full_name: Gynecologic Oncology Group Study 173: Lymphatic Mapping and Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy in Women with Squamous Cell Carcinoma of the Vulva
key_trials.population: Females with vulva-confined primary tumors 2–6 cm, ≥1 mm invasion, clinically node negative
key_trials.intervention: SLN mapping and biopsy followed by inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy
key_trials.primary_endpoint: Sensitivity and false-negative rate of SLNB
key_trials.key_result: SLN detection rate 92.5% (418/452); sensitivity 91.7%; NPV 96.3%; false-negative predictive value 3.7% overall, 2% for tumors <4 cm
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Confirmed that SLNB can replace full lymphadenectomy in select patients with tumors <4 cm
key_trials.practice_change: Supported SLNB as standard for early-stage vulvar cancer with clinically negative groins and tumors <4 cm.

key_trials.full_name: Phase II Trial of Cisplatin, Gemcitabine, and Intensity-Modulated Radiation Therapy for Locally Advanced Vulvar Squamous Cell Carcinoma (NRG Oncology/GOG Study 279)
key_trials.population: Patients with locally advanced vulvar SCC not amenable to surgical resection
key_trials.intervention: Weekly cisplatin (40 mg/m2) + gemcitabine (50 mg/m2) concurrent with IMRT
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrated high pCR with cisplatin/gemcitabine/IMRT in locally advanced vulvar cancer; provides an effective chemoradiation option.

key_trials.full_name: Phase II KEYNOTE-158 Study: Pembrolizumab in Previously Treated Advanced Vulvar Squamous Cell Carcinoma
key_trials.population: Patients with advanced vulvar SCC that progressed after standard therapy
key_trials.key_result: ORR 10.9% overall; 9.5% in PD-L1-positive (CPS ≥1); 28.6% in PD-L1-negative. Median PFS 2.1 months, median OS 6.2 months
key_trials.practice_change: Pembrolizumab is included as a second-line option for PD-L1-positive vulvar cancer (CPS ≥1) and for TMB-H or MSI-H/dMMR tumors based on other cohorts in the same trial.

key_trials.acronym: Key Trials: GOG 101 and GOG 205 (combined summary)
key_trials.full_name: GOG 101: Preoperative chemoradiation for advanced vulvar cancer; GOG 205: Phase II trial of RT and weekly cisplatin for locally advanced vulvar cancer
key_trials.journal: International Journal of Radiation Oncology, Biology, Physics (GOG 101); Gynecologic Oncology (GOG 205)
key_trials.intervention: GOG 101: Preoperative cisplatin/5-FU + RT; GOG 205: Weekly cisplatin + RT
key_trials.key_result: GOG 101: 3% residual unresectable disease, 96% preserved urinary/GI continence. GOG 205: Complete clinical response 64%, pCR 78% among those biopsied
key_trials.practice_change: Established chemoradiation as standard for locally advanced vulvar cancer, allowing organ preservation and avoidance of exenteration.
